Professor View 01 / 42
Travel & Social English -- 21 Aulas

Tuca Dias

From "it feels like there is a gap" to being yourself in English, curious, conversing, asking questions, wherever your travels take you.

B1 S. S. da Grama (SP) / Pocos de Caldas (MG) Coffee Farm Owner 60 min / Presencial / 2x semana
Progresso Geral0%
Speed:
Tuca Dias
61 anos
Produtora de café especial
São Sebastião da Grama (SP) / Poços de Caldas (MG) / São Paulo (SP)
B1 (Intermediário)
Travel & Social English
21 aulas de 60 min
Presencial, 2x/semana

Jornada de Transformação

De: Viajante curiosa que sente "um gap" entre o que quer dizer e o que consegue expressar em inglês. Evita conversas mais profundas por insegurança.

Para: Viajante confiante que conversa com naturalidade em hotéis, restaurantes e com pessoas de outras culturas. Faz perguntas, conta histórias e compartilha sua vida e seu café com o mundo.

Promessa do Programa

"From 'it feels like there is a gap' to being yourself in English, curious, conversing, asking questions, wherever your travels take you."

Forças

  • Curiosidade natural e amor por viagens e culturas
  • Experiência de vida rica, muitas histórias para contar
  • Autoconhecimento linguístico, sabe onde precisa melhorar
  • Motivação alta: viagens reais planejadas (México, Japão, Mont Blanc)
  • Vocabulário passivo razoável, compreende mais do que produz

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Produção oral: hesitação e frases incompletas
  • Confusão entre present simple e past simple
  • Vocabulário ativo limitado para situações sociais
  • Pronúncia de palavras com "th", "r" e vogais longas
  • Dificuldade em manter conversação por mais de 2-3 trocas

Currículo -- 21 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Who Is Tuca? -- Diagnostic + Personal StorytellingPresent simple + Past simple (diagnostic)Map activity + storytellingWrite 8-10 sentences about last trip + record 90s audio
2At the Airport -- Navigating DeparturesPresent continuous + Going toAirport simulation role-playListen to airport announcements podcast
3Have You Ever? -- Travel ExperiencesPresent perfect (life experiences)Experience sharing + dialogueWrite 5 "Have you ever..." questions + record answers
4Street Smart -- Asking for DirectionsImperatives + Prepositions of placeCity navigation role-playRecord directions to your coffee farm
5Table Talk -- Ordering & Food CultureWould like + Countable/uncountableRestaurant role-play (Mexican cuisine)Describe 3 Brazilian dishes in English
6Small Talk -- Starting ConversationsPresent perfect (experiences)Small talk at a café role-playStart 3 conversations with strangers (practice)
7Culture Curious -- Asking Deep QuestionsQuestion forms + Past simpleInterview a foreigner role-playWrite 10 questions about Japanese culture
8Mexico City -- Pre-trip ImmersionPast simple vs Present perfectMexico City day plan simulationResearch 5 places to visit in Mexico City
9On the Trail -- Mont Blanc PreparationFuture forms (will/going to)Trekking equipment & safety role-playWrite packing list with descriptions
10Progress Check -- The Story So FarAll structures (review)Re-record intro + compare with Aula 1Listen to both recordings & reflect
11Getting Around -- Public TransportModal verbs (should/could/might)Transport problem-solving role-playDescribe public transport in your city
12Emergency! -- Health & Safety AbroadFirst conditional + Must/have toEmergency situation simulationWrite emergency card in English
13Market Day -- Shopping & NegotiatingComparatives + SuperlativesMarket haggling role-playCompare prices: Brazil vs abroad
14Tell Me More -- Storytelling SkillsPast continuous + When/WhileTell the Ruta 60+ storyRecord the Ruta 60+ story in English
15Coffee Talk -- Explaining Your WorldPassive voice (introduction)Explain coffee production processWrite about your farm for visitors
16Japan Prep -- Cultural AwarenessSecond conditionalJapanese customs role-playResearch Japanese etiquette
17At the Museum -- Art & HistoryUsed to + Would (past habits)Museum tour guide role-playDescribe a favorite piece of art
18Making Plans -- Social InvitationsSuggestions (How about/Why don't we)Planning a day in Tokyo role-playWrite invitation email to a friend
19Deep Conversations -- Opinions & ValuesAgree/Disagree + In my opinionDebate on cultural topicsWrite opinion paragraph
20The Confident Traveler -- IntegrationAll structures integratedFull day abroad simulationFinal reflection journal
21Tuca in English -- Final CelebrationAll structures + PresentationTell your story: who you are in EnglishRecord final story (compare with Aula 1)

Welcome, Tuca!

Before your first lesson, practice these emergency phrases. They will help you during class if you need a moment.

1 Could you repeat that, please?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain?
3 Let me think about that for a moment.
4 I am from Brazil. I have a coffee farm.
5 Could you speak a little slower, please?
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Tuca?

Diagnostic conversation, personal storytelling, and travel vocabulary
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen, read, and learn these 10 essential travel words.

Journey -- a long trip from one place to another
"My journey to Morocco was unforgettable."
Destination -- the place where someone is going or being sent
"Our destination was a small village in the mountains."
Local -- relating to or found in a particular area
"I always try to eat local food when I travel."
Culture -- the customs, beliefs, art, and way of life of a group of people
"I love learning about the culture of new places."
Adventure -- an exciting or unusual experience
"Traveling is always an adventure."
Explore -- to travel to a place to learn about it
"I want to explore the streets of Tokyo."
Border -- the line that divides two countries or areas
"We crossed the border between Spain and Portugal."
Route -- the way you go from one place to another
"What is the best route to the airport?"
Experience -- something that happens to you or something you do
"That was an amazing experience."
International -- involving two or more countries
"She works for an international company."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Vocabulary

Match each word with its definition.

Journey
Destination
Local
Culture
Adventure
Explore
Border
Route
Experience
International

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the text and answer the comprehension questions.

Anna arrived in Marrakech after a long journey from London. Her destination was a small riad in the old city. She explores the local markets every morning and tries traditional food. The culture is very different from home. Every day is a new adventure. Yesterday, she crossed the border into the desert and followed a new route. The experience changed her life. She travels to international destinations every year because she loves to explore.
1. Which verb tense describes what Anna does every day?
A Present simple (explores, tries, travels)
B Past simple (arrived, crossed)
C Future simple (will travel)
2. "She crossed the border into the desert" uses which tense?
A Present simple
B Past simple
C Present continuous
3. What was Anna's destination?
A A hotel in London
B A small riad in the old city of Marrakech
C A restaurant in the desert

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = habits, routines, facts that are always true.
Past Simple = completed actions in the past, with a specific time.

TenseFormulaExample
Present Simple (+)Subject + verb (base form / -s)"I travel every year." / "She grows coffee."
Present Simple (-)Subject + do/does + not + verb"I do not travel alone."
Present Simple (?)Do/Does + subject + verb?"Do you like traveling?"
Past Simple (+)Subject + verb (past form)"I visited Mexico last year."
Past Simple (-)Subject + did + not + verb (base)"I did not visit Japan."
Past Simple (?)Did + subject + verb (base)?"Did you enjoy the trip?"

Key Patterns for Travel

  • "I usually..." + present simple (habits)
  • "Last year I..." + past simple (completed action)
  • "The most interesting thing was..." + past simple (experience)
  • "I live in..." + present simple (fact)
  • "I met..." + past simple (specific event)

Common Mistake:

"I go to Mexico last year."

"I went to Mexico last year."

With "last year," "yesterday," or any specific past time, always use the past simple form of the verb.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"My to Morocco took about twelve hours."
"Our was a small village in the mountains."
"I always try to eat food when I travel."
"I love learning about the of new places."
"Last summer I the south of France."
"We crossed the between Spain and Portugal."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put this airport conversation in the correct order.

? "Here is my boarding pass and passport."
? "Good morning! Where are you flying to today?"
? "Thank you! Have a nice flight!"
? "Your seat is 14A, a window seat. Gate 12."
? "I am going to Mexico City. Can I check in here?"

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, record, and compare your pronunciation.

I am from Brazil. I have a coffee farm.
I grow specialty coffee in a small town.
Last year I went to Mexico City with my family.
We explored the local markets and tried amazing food.
The culture was very rich and different from Brazil.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You meet someone at a hotel. They ask "Where are you from?" What is the best answer?
A "I from Brazil."
B "I am from Brazil. I live in a small town in Sao Paulo state."
C "Brazil is my country."
2. Someone asks "What do you do?" What is the best response for Tuca?
A "I do coffee."
B "I am coffee farmer."
C "I have a coffee farm in Brazil. I grow specialty coffee."
3. You want to describe a past trip. Which sentence is correct?
A "Last year I visited Morocco and explored the local markets."
B "Last year I visit Morocco and explore the local markets."
C "Last year I am visiting Morocco."
4. You want to say what you normally do. Which is correct?
A "I am usually travel once a year."
B "I usually travel once or twice a year."
C "I usually traveled once a year."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at a hotel lobby in Morocco. A traveler from the United States asks: "Tell me about yourself! Where are you from? What do you do?" Record yourself answering. Try to use: present simple for your life now, past simple for a trip you took, and at least 3 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1 I am from Brazil. I have a coffee farm.
2 I love traveling and meeting new people.
3 Last year I visited Morocco. It was incredible.
4 I usually travel once or twice a year.
5 Could you repeat that, please?
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

At the Airport

Airport vocabulary, present continuous, going to, and check-in conversations
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen, read, and learn these 10 essential airport words.

Boarding pass -- a card that shows your seat number and flight details
"Please have your boarding pass ready before you reach the gate."
Check-in -- the process of registering for your flight at the airport
"The check-in counter closes 45 minutes before departure."
Gate -- the area where passengers wait and board the plane
"All passengers for Flight AM 742, please proceed to Gate 22."
Departure -- when a plane leaves the airport
"The departure time has been changed to 11:15."
Luggage -- bags and suitcases you take when traveling
"You can pick up your luggage at the baggage claim area."
Delay -- when something happens later than expected
"There is a 30-minute delay due to weather conditions."
Announcement -- an official message spoken over a loudspeaker
"Listen to the announcement for your gate number."
Terminal -- a large building at the airport for arrivals and departures
"Terminal 3 is for international flights."
Carry-on -- a small bag you take on the plane with you
"Your carry-on must fit under the seat in front of you."
Security -- the checkpoint where officers check bags and passengers
"Please remove your laptop at the security checkpoint."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Boarding pass
Check-in
Gate
Departure
Luggage
Delay
Announcement
Terminal
Carry-on
Security

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the text below. Pay attention to the words in bold.

Tuca is standing at the check-in counter at the airport. She is going to fly to Mexico City today. The agent is looking at her passport. Tuca is checking one suitcase and is carrying a small bag as her carry-on. Her flight is departing from Gate 22, but there is going to be a small delay. Tuca is not worried. She is going to wait at the gate and read her book. The airport is making an announcement about her flight right now.
1. What is Tuca doing at the airport right now?
A She is buying a ticket.
B She is standing at the check-in counter.
C She is boarding the plane.
2. What is Tuca going to do about the delay?
A She is going to complain.
B She is going to change her flight.
C She is going to wait at the gate and read.
3. The bold words describe actions happening NOW or future PLANS. Which grammar structures are used?
A Past simple and present simple
B Present continuous and going to
C Past continuous and will

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Continuous = Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing. Use it for actions happening right now.

Going to = Subject + am/is/are + going to + verb. Use it for future plans and intentions.

FormPresent ContinuousGoing to
+I am waiting at the gate.I am going to fly to Mexico.
-She is not checking luggage.He is not going to check luggage.
?Are you waiting at the gate?Are you going to fly today?
Common Mistake: "I am go to the airport." This is wrong because present continuous needs -ing: "I am going to the airport." And for future plans: "I am going to go to the airport."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form.

"I at the gate right now."
"She to Mexico City tomorrow."
"They the plane now."
"The flight in one hour."
"We at 3 PM."
"He for his boarding pass right now."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put this airport check-in conversation in the correct order.

? "Here you go. I checked in online last night."
? "Good morning! Where are you flying to today?"
? "Yes, I have one suitcase. And this is my carry-on."
? "Great! Can I see your passport and boarding pass, please?"
? "Your luggage is all set. Your flight is departing from Gate 22."
? "Good morning! I am going to Mexico City."
? "Perfect. Are you checking any luggage today?"
? "No problem. I am going to wait at the gate. Thank you!"

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I would like to check in for my flight, please.
Excuse me, where is Gate 22?
Is there a delay on Flight AM 742?
Can I take this bag as a carry-on?
My boarding pass says Gate 22 but I heard an announcement about a change.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each airport situation.

1. You arrive at the airport and need to get your boarding pass. What do you say?
A "I would like to check in for my flight, please."
B "Where is the restaurant?"
C "I need a taxi to the hotel."
2. You hear an announcement but cannot find your gate. What do you ask?
A "What time is it?"
B "Excuse me, where is Gate 22?"
C "How much is this coffee?"
3. The security officer asks you to open your bag. What is the most natural response?
A "No, thank you."
B "I do not have a bag."
C "Of course. Here you go."
4. Your flight is delayed. You want to know the new time. What do you ask?
A "Where is the plane?"
B "Is there a delay? What is the new departure time?"
C "I want to go home."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection

Record yourself checking in at an airport. Include: your name, your destination, and your luggage.

Imagine you are at the check-in counter. Tell the agent: your name, where you are flying, if you have luggage, and ask about your gate.
Suggested answer: "Good morning! My name is Tuca Dias. I am going to Mexico City. I have one suitcase to check and this is my carry-on. Could you tell me which gate my flight departs from? Thank you!"

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1 I would like to check in for my flight, please.
2 Excuse me, where is Gate 22?
3 Is there a delay on my flight?
4 Can I take this bag as a carry-on?
5 Where is the nearest security checkpoint?
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

Have You Ever?

Present perfect for life experiences, travel history, and cultural encounters
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen, read, and learn these 8 new words about travel experiences.

Cuisine -- the style of cooking from a particular country or region
"The cuisine in Thailand is spicy and diverse."
Nomadic -- moving from place to place instead of staying in one location
"Some people live a nomadic lifestyle, always moving."
Diverse -- showing a great deal of variety; very different
"Brazil is a very diverse country with many cultures."
Unforgettable -- impossible to forget; very memorable
"Meeting that family was an unforgettable experience."
Encounter -- an unexpected meeting with someone
"I had a wonderful encounter with a local artist."
Fascinating -- extremely interesting and attractive
"The history of that city is absolutely fascinating."
Connection -- a feeling of understanding between people
"I felt a deep connection with the people there."
Humble -- not proud; simple and modest
"The family was humble but incredibly generous."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its correct definition.

Unforgettable
Cuisine
Nomadic
Fascinating
Diverse
Encounter
Connection
Humble

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the text and answer the questions below.

Sofia is a travel writer from Portugal. She has traveled to 30 countries in the last ten years. The most unforgettable experience she has ever had was in Peru. She has tried the local cuisine in every country she has visited. She has never been disappointed. The most fascinating encounter she has ever had was with a nomadic family in Mongolia. They were so humble and generous. She has always believed that travel creates connections that nothing else can. Sofia says: "I have never regretted a single trip. Every journey is worth it."

1. How many countries has Sofia visited?
A 20 countries
B 30 countries
C 10 countries
2. Where was Sofia's most fascinating encounter?
A Peru
B Portugal
C Mongolia
3. What verb form does Sofia use to talk about her life experiences?
A Past simple (visited, tried)
B Present perfect (has traveled, has tried)
C Present continuous (is traveling, is trying)

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Present Perfect for Life Experiences

Use the present perfect (have/has + past participle) to talk about life experiences when the exact time does not matter.

FormStructureExample
+Subject + have/has + past participleI have visited 20 countries.
-Subject + have/has + not + past participleI have never been to Spain.
?Have/Has + subject + past participleHave you ever been to Asia?
Trigger Words

ever (questions) | never (negative experiences) | already (done before expected) | yet (not done, expected) | before (at some point in the past) | so far (up to now)

Common Mistake

Present Perfect = life experience (no specific time): "I have been to Japan."
Past Simple = specific moment: "I went to Japan last year."
WRONG: "I have been to Japan last year."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form.

"Have you ever to Spain?"
"I have tried Japanese food."
"She has more than 30 countries."
"We have always to go to Patagonia."
"The in Thailand is spicy and diverse."
"I felt a deep with the people there."

Stage 2: Conversation Order

Order

Put this conversation at a cultural event in the correct order.

? "I am from Brazil. I have visited about 20 countries so far."
? "No, I have never been to Spain, but it is on my bucket list!"
? "This is a wonderful event. Have you been to one like this before?"
? "You should visit! The cuisine is unforgettable."
? "No, this is my first time. But I have always wanted to attend a cultural festival."
? "That is impressive! Have you ever been to Spain?"

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, then record yourself saying each phrase.

I have visited about 20 countries so far.
Have you ever tried local cuisine in Asia?
I have never been to a cultural festival before.
I have always wanted to attend a cultural festival.
I ended up making a wonderful connection with a local family.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You meet someone at a travel event and want to ask about their experiences. What do you say?
A "Did you ever traveled to Asia?"
B "Have you ever been to Asia?"
C "Are you been to Asia before?"
2. Someone asks about your travel history. You want to say you have not visited Japan yet. What do you say?
A "I did not visited Japan yet."
B "I have not visited Japan yet."
C "I am not visiting Japan yet."
3. You want to tell a new friend that Peru is a dream destination. What do you say?
A "Peru has always been on my bucket list."
B "Peru is always been on my bucket list."
C "Peru was always on my bucket list since before."
4. You want to describe a trip that was deeply memorable. What do you say?
A "It was a once-in-a-life experience."
B "That was a once-in-a-lifetime experience."
C "That is a once-in-a-life experience."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Think about your most unforgettable travel experience. Where did you go? Who did you meet? What made it special? Record yourself speaking for about 1 minute using the present perfect: "I have been to...", "I have never...", "I have always wanted to..."

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1 Have you ever been to...?
2 I have always wanted to...
3 That was a once-in-a-lifetime experience.
4 I ended up staying three more days.
5 I felt a deep connection with the people there.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

Street Smart -- Asking for Directions

Imperatives (Go, Turn, Cross, Don't cross) + prepositions of place (next to, opposite, between, in front of, on the corner of) for asking and giving directions. New vocabulary: intersection, roundabout, block, landmark, crosswalk, sidewalk, corner, traffic light, straight ahead, avenue, opposite, square.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Intersection -- the point where two streets cross
"Turn left at the next intersection." (Cruzamento)
Roundabout -- a circular junction where roads meet
"Take the second exit at the roundabout." (Rotatória)
Block -- the section of a street between two corners
"Walk two blocks and you will see it." (Quarteirão)
Landmark -- a well-known place used as a reference
"The old church is a useful landmark." (Ponto de referência)
Crosswalk -- the marked place where you cross the street
"Cross at the crosswalk, not in the middle." (Faixa de pedestres)
Sidewalk -- the path beside the road for people walking
"Stay on this sidewalk and keep going." (Calçada)
Corner -- the place where two streets meet
"The shop is right on the corner." (Esquina)
Traffic light -- the signal that controls cars at a junction
"Turn right at the traffic light." (Semáforo)
Straight ahead -- directly forward, without turning
"Go straight ahead for two blocks." (Em frente)
Avenue -- a wide street in a city
"The shop is on a busy avenue." (Avenida)
Opposite -- directly across from something
"The bank is opposite the square." (Em frente a)
Square -- an open public space in a city
"Let's meet in the main square." (Praça)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Intersection
Roundabout
Block
Landmark
Crosswalk
Sidewalk
Corner
Traffic light
Avenue
Square

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca was lost downtown, so she asked a local for directions. He said: "Go straight ahead for two blocks until you reach the avenue. Turn left at the traffic light and cross at the crosswalk. Stay on the same sidewalk and walk past the square. The coffee shop is on the corner, next to a bookstore and opposite the old church. Don't go as far as the roundabout -- if you reach it, you have gone too far." The old church was the perfect landmark, so Tuca found the shop in five minutes.

1. Which sentence is a correct imperative for giving a direction?
A "You going straight ahead for two blocks."
B "Go straight ahead for two blocks."
C "To go straight ahead for two blocks."
2. Where is the coffee shop?
A On the corner, next to a bookstore and opposite the old church.
B At the roundabout, behind the square.
C Between the avenue and the traffic light.
3. What does "Don't go as far as the roundabout" tell us?
A The roundabout is too far; reaching it means she missed the shop.
B The shop is at the roundabout.
C She should cross at the roundabout.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Imperatives & Prepositions of Place

GRAMMAR

Usados para dar e localizar direcoes. / Used to give directions and locate places.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
Imperative (base verb)Ordem ou direcao, sem sujeito / A command, no subjectGo straight. Turn left. Cross the street.
Negative imperativeDizer para NAO fazer algo / Tell someone NOT to do itDon't cross here. Don't turn yet.
Prepositions of placeMostram a posicao de um lugar / Show where a place isIt's next to / opposite / between the bank.
Key point / Ponto-chaveDiga "Turn left", nunca "Turn to left". E sempre "in front of", nunca "in front the".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Go straight ahead and turn left at the ."
"Cross the street at the ."
"The coffee shop is on the ."
"The bank is the square."
"Walk two and you will see the avenue."
"The old church is a useful ."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?The local tells her to go straight ahead and turn at the traffic light.
?Tuca leaves her hotel and realizes she does not know the way.
?She arrives at the coffee shop, right next to the bookstore.
?She stops a friendly local and asks for directions.
?Tuca walks past the square and finds the corner.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the station?
Com licença, você poderia me dizer como chegar à estação?
Go straight ahead and turn left at the corner.
Siga em frente e vire à esquerda na esquina.
The shop is on the corner, opposite the church.
A loja fica na esquina, em frente à igreja.
I think I'm lost. Am I going the right way?
Acho que estou perdida. Estou indo na direção certa?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each travel situation.

You stop someone on the street to ask for directions. How do you start politely?
A "Hey! Where is the station, fast."
B "Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the station?"
C "You take me to the station now."
You want to tell a tourist to go forward without turning. Which is correct?
A "Go straight ahead for two blocks."
B "You go to straight two blocks."
C "Going straight ahead two blocks."
The hotel faces the bank across the street. How do you describe its position?
A "The hotel is in front the bank."
B "The hotel is opposite the bank."
C "The hotel is next the bank."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a foreign visitor is at your hotel and wants to reach your coffee farm. Give clear directions: use at least three imperatives (Go, Turn, Cross) and three prepositions of place (next to, opposite, between, on the corner of). Mention at least one landmark.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1Excuse me, I'm looking for this place.Com licença, estou procurando este lugar.
2Could you tell me how to get there?Você poderia me dizer como chegar lá?
3Go straight ahead and turn left at the corner.Siga em frente e vire à esquerda na esquina.
4It's on the corner, opposite the church.Fica na esquina, em frente à igreja.
5Am I going the right way?Estou indo na direção certa?
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

Table Talk -- Ordering & Food Culture

Ordering politely with "would like" + countable and uncountable nouns (some, much, many, a little, a few) at a restaurant. New vocabulary: appetizer, main course, side dish, dessert, beverage, spicy, refreshing, recommendation, bill, tip, server, flavor.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Appetizer -- a small dish served before the main course
"We ordered guacamole as an appetizer." (Entrada)
Main course -- the largest, central dish of a meal
"For the main course, I had chicken tacos." (Prato principal)
Side dish -- a smaller dish served with the main course
"Rice and beans are a great side dish." (Acompanhamento)
Dessert -- a sweet dish eaten at the end of a meal
"For dessert, we shared some churros." (Sobremesa)
Beverage -- a drink, especially one other than water
"What beverage would you like with your meal?" (Bebida)
Spicy -- having a strong, hot flavor from peppers or spices
"The green sauce is very spicy." (Apimentado)
Refreshing -- pleasantly cool and reviving to drink
"The lime water was so refreshing." (Refrescante)
Recommendation -- a suggestion about something good to choose
"I followed the server's recommendation." (Recomendação)
Bill -- the paper showing how much you must pay
"Could we have the bill, please?" (Conta)
Tip -- extra money you leave for good service
"I left a good tip for the server." (Gorjeta)
Server -- the person who takes your order and brings the food
"The server was very friendly." (Garçom / Garçonete)
Flavor -- the taste of a food or drink
"The flavor of the churros was amazing." (Sabor)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Appetizer
Main course
Side dish
Dessert
Beverage
Spicy
Refreshing
Recommendation
Bill
Tip
Server
Flavor

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca sat down at a Mexican restaurant and the server brought the menu. "I would like to start with the guacamole appetizer, please," she said. The server asked, "Would you like a side dish with your main course?" Tuca answered, "Yes, I'd like some rice and a few tortillas." Because rice is uncountable, she said "some rice", but tortillas are countable, so she said "a few tortillas". She did not want too much chili, so she added, "Just a little spicy sauce, please." For a beverage, she chose a refreshing lime water, and she saved room for dessert.

1. Which sentence is the polite way to order?
A "I want the guacamole appetizer."
B "I would like the guacamole appetizer, please."
C "Give me the guacamole appetizer."
2. Why does Tuca say "a few tortillas" but "some rice"?
A Tortillas are countable, but rice is uncountable.
B Rice is countable, but tortillas are uncountable.
C There is no difference; both are correct either way.
3. How does Tuca ask for a small amount of sauce?
A "Just a few spicy sauce, please."
B "Just a little spicy sauce, please."
C "Just many spicy sauce, please."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- "Would like" & Countable/Uncountable

GRAMMAR

Usados para pedir com educacao e medir quantidades. / Used to order politely and measure quantities.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
Would like (+)Pedido educado / A polite requestI would like / I'd like a coffee, please.
Would like (?)Oferecer algo / Offer somethingWould you like a dessert?
Countable (contaveis)Pode contar: a/an, some, many, a few / You can count thema taco, two tacos, a few tortillas, How many?
Uncountable (incontaveis)Nao se conta: some, much, a little / You cannot count themsome rice, a little water, How much?

Key point / Ponto-chave: Use "How many?" para contaveis (tacos) e "How much?" para incontaveis (rice, water). E sempre "I'd like", nunca "I want", para soar educado.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"To start, I would like the guacamole ."
"For the , I will have the chicken tacos."
"Could I have rice as a ?"
"How tacos would you like?"
"Could I have water, please?"
"Excuse me, could we have the , please?"

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?The server suggests the guacamole and the chicken tacos.
?Tuca arrives at the Mexican restaurant and the server gives her a menu.
?After the meal, she asks for the bill and leaves a tip.
?She asks the server for a recommendation.
?Tuca orders her main course and a refreshing beverage.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Could I see the menu, please?
Eu poderia ver o cardápio, por favor?
I'd like the chicken tacos for my main course.
Eu gostaria dos tacos de frango como prato principal.
What would you recommend?
O que você recomendaria?
Could we have the bill, please?
Poderíamos ter a conta, por favor?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each restaurant situation.

The server hands you the menu. How do you politely order a dish?
A "Give me the tacos now."
B "I'd like the chicken tacos, please."
C "I want tacos, fast."
You want to know the quantity of a countable item. Which question is correct?
A "How many tacos would you like?"
B "How much tacos would you like?"
C "How many taco would you like?"
You want a small amount of an uncountable item. How do you ask?
A "Could I have a few water?"
B "Could I have a little water?"
C "Could I have many water?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are ordering a full meal at a Mexican restaurant. Order an appetizer, a main course with a side dish, a beverage, and a dessert. Use "I would like" at least three times, and use "How much" or "How many" at least once. Then describe one Brazilian dish you would recommend to the server.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Could I see the menu, please?Eu poderia ver o cardápio, por favor?
2I'd like the chicken tacos, please.Eu gostaria dos tacos de frango, por favor.
3What do you recommend?O que você recomenda?
4Could I have some water, please?Eu poderia ter um pouco de água, por favor?
5Could we have the bill, please?Poderíamos ter a conta, por favor?
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

Small Talk -- Starting Conversations

Making small talk with the present perfect (Have you ever...?, for / since) to start and keep a friendly conversation. New vocabulary: small talk, icebreaker, acquaintance, outgoing, awkward, chat, get along, in common, conversation starter, polite, topic, bring up.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Small talk -- light, friendly conversation about everyday topics
"We made small talk about the coffee while we waited." (Conversa casual)
Icebreaker -- something you say to start a conversation comfortably
"A question about the weather is a great icebreaker." (Quebra-gelo)
Acquaintance -- a person you know a little, but not well
"He is an acquaintance from my coffee class." (Conhecido)
Outgoing -- friendly and confident about meeting new people
"She is very outgoing and talks to everyone." (Extrovertido)
Awkward -- uncomfortable, when you are not sure what to say
"There was an awkward silence after my question." (Constrangedor)
Chat -- a relaxed, informal conversation
"We had a nice chat over coffee." (Bate-papo)
Get along -- to have a friendly relationship with someone
"I get along with almost everyone at the cafe." (Se dar bem)
In common -- shared between people, like interests or experiences
"We have a love of travel in common." (Em comum)
Conversation starter -- a topic or question that begins a conversation
"Travel is always a good conversation starter." (Assunto inicial)
Polite -- showing good manners and respect to other people
"It is polite to ask about the other person too." (Educado)
Topic -- the subject you are talking about
"The weather is a safe topic with strangers." (Tema)
Bring up -- to start talking about a subject
"Do not bring up money with new people." (Mencionar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Small talk
Icebreaker
Acquaintance
Outgoing
Awkward
Chat
Get along
In common
Conversation starter
Polite
Topic
Bring up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca walked into a busy cafe for a language exchange. To break the ice, she made some small talk about the coffee. "Have you ever been to this cafe before?" she asked a friendly stranger. "Yes, I have. I have come here every week for two years," he answered. Tuca noticed they had a love of travel in common, so she asked a follow-up question: "How long have you studied English?" They got along very well. Because the present perfect connects the past to now, it is the perfect tool for small talk: you ask "Have you ever...?" for experiences, and you use for and since to say how long something has continued.

1. Which question asks about a life experience?
A "Have you ever been to this cafe?"
B "Are you at the cafe now?"
C "Will you go to the cafe?"
2. Why does the man say "for two years"?
A because "for" is used with a point in time
B because "for" shows a period of time
C because the action is finished and over
3. What is the present perfect good for in small talk?
A asking about experiences with "Have you ever...?"
B giving orders and commands
C describing the weather right now

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect (ever / for / since)

GRAMMAR

Usado para falar de experiencias e iniciar conversas. / Used to talk about experiences and start conversations.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
Present perfect (+)have/has + particio passado / links past to nowI have visited many cafes.
Have you ever...? (?)Pergunta sobre experiencia / asks about a life experienceHave you ever been to Mexico?
for (+ periodo)Duracao / a period of timeI have lived here for two years.
since (+ ponto)Ponto de inicio / a starting point in timeI have lived here since 2020.

Key point / Ponto-chave: Use "Have you ever...?" para perguntar sobre experiencias. Use for + periodo (two years) e since + ponto de inicio (Monday). Nunca use o presente simples ("I am here since...") -- use o present perfect.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" you ever been to a language exchange?"
"A simple question is a great to start talking."
"We both love coffee, so we have something in ."
"I have lived in this city two years."
"I have been here Monday."
"If there is an awkward silence, up a new topic."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?She asks him an experience question with "Have you ever...?"
?Tuca arrives at the cafe and sits down next to a stranger.
?Before leaving, they agree to meet again next Thursday.
?She breaks the ice with some small talk about the coffee.
?They discover they both love coffee and have a lot in common.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Hi, is this seat free?
Oi, esse lugar está livre?
Have you ever traveled abroad?
Você já viajou para o exterior?
We have a lot in common.
Nós temos muito em comum.
It has been great chatting with you.
Foi ótimo conversar com você.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each small-talk situation.

You sit down next to a stranger. How do you break the ice politely?
A "Move over, please."
B "Hi, is this seat free? Have you been here before?"
C "Why are you here?"
You want to ask about a life experience. Which is correct?
A "Did you ever been abroad?"
B "Have you ever been abroad?"
C "Are you ever abroad?"
Someone says they love coffee, like you. How do you keep the chat going?
A "OK."
B "Me too! What kinds have you tried?"
C "I have to go now."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are at a cafe and you want to start a conversation with a stranger. Break the ice with an icebreaker, ask at least two experience questions with "Have you ever...?", find something you have in common, and keep the conversation going with follow-up questions. Use at least three new words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1Hi, is this seat free?Oi, esse lugar está livre?
2Have you been here before?Você já veio aqui antes?
3Have you ever traveled abroad?Você já viajou para o exterior?
4We have a lot in common!Nós temos muito em comum!
5It has been great chatting with you.Foi ótimo conversar com você.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

Culture Curious -- Asking Deep Questions

Asking great questions about a person's culture and past with question forms and the past simple (Where did you...?, What did you...?). New vocabulary: custom, tradition, heritage, background, belief, foreigner, native, lifestyle, etiquette, curious, greeting, misunderstanding.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Custom -- a usual way of doing something in a culture or group
"Taking off your shoes at the door is a common custom." (Costume)
Tradition -- a belief or action passed down through generations
"A family dinner on Sundays is an old tradition." (Tradição)
Heritage -- the history and traditions a group receives from the past
"She is proud of her Japanese heritage." (Herança)
Background -- where a person comes from; their family and origins
"He comes from a farming background, like me." (Origem)
Belief -- something you accept as true or feel is real
"Respect for elders is an important belief there." (Crença)
Foreigner -- a person from another country
"As a foreigner, I was curious about local customs." (Estrangeiro)
Native -- belonging to a place because you were born there
"He is a native of Kyoto and speaks the local dialect." (Nativo)
Lifestyle -- the way a person lives, including their habits and choices
"Living on a coffee farm gives me a calm lifestyle." (Estilo de vida)
Etiquette -- the polite rules of behavior in a society or situation
"Table etiquette is different in every country." (Etiqueta)
Curious -- wanting to learn or know more about something
"I am very curious about other cultures." (Curioso)
Greeting -- what you say or do when you meet someone
"A bow is a common greeting in Japan." (Saudação)
Misunderstanding -- a failure to understand something correctly
"It was just a small misunderstanding about the custom." (Mal-entendido)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Custom
Tradition
Heritage
Background
Belief
Foreigner
Native
Lifestyle
Etiquette
Curious
Greeting
Misunderstanding

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca was very curious about other cultures, so she interviewed a foreigner named Kenji about his background. She prepared her questions carefully. "Where did you grow up?" she asked. "I grew up in Kyoto," he answered. "What traditions did your family celebrate?" He told her about the festivals and the etiquette of the tea ceremony, part of his heritage. To build a good past question, Tuca remembered the order: question word, then did, then the subject, then the base verb. The word "did" already shows the past, so the main verb stays in its base form: "Did you like living abroad?", not "Did you liked."

1. Which question is built correctly?
A "Where did you grow up?"
B "Where you grew up?"
C "Where did you grew up?"
2. Why does the main verb stay in the base form?
A because the question is about the future
B because "did" already shows the past
C because base verbs are always polite
3. What is the correct order of a past Wh- question?
A question word + did + subject + base verb
B subject + did + question word + verb
C did + question word + verb + subject

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Simple Questions

GRAMMAR

Usado para perguntar sobre o passado e entrevistar alguém. / Used to ask about the past and interview someone.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
Wh- questionWh-word + did + sujeito + verbo base / asks for informationWhere did you grow up?
Yes / No questionDid + sujeito + verbo base / sim ou nãoDid you like the food?
Verbo principalforma base, porque "did" já marca o passadogo (não went), grow (não grew)
Wh-wordspara abrir a perguntaWhat, Where, When, Why, How

Key point / Ponto-chave: Monte a pergunta na ordem: question word + did + subject + base verb. O "did" já mostra o passado, então o verbo principal fica na forma base ("Did you go?", nunca "Did you went?").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Where you grow up?"
"I am very about other cultures."
"A bow is a common in Japan."
"What traditions your family celebrate?"
"It was just a small about the custom."
"She is very proud of her Japanese ."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?She asked where he grew up and what his family celebrated.
?Tuca wanted to learn about a culture, so she met Kenji from Japan.
?Tuca thanked him for sharing his heritage.
?She prepared ten questions about his background and traditions.
?They cleared up a small misunderstanding about a custom.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Where did you grow up?
Onde você cresceu?
What traditions are important to you?
Quais tradições são importantes para você?
I am curious about your culture.
Estou curiosa sobre a sua cultura.
Thank you for sharing your heritage.
Obrigada por compartilhar a sua herança.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each interview situation.

You want to ask a foreigner about the place they grew up. Which is correct?
A "Where you grew up?"
B "Where did you grow up?"
C "Where did you grew up?"
You want to ask about the traditions their family celebrated. Which is correct?
A "What did your family celebrate?"
B "What your family celebrated?"
C "What did your family celebrated?"
A foreigner shares a custom you did not know. How do you react politely?
A "That is strange."
B "How interesting! Why is it important?"
C "I do not care about that."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are interviewing a foreigner about their culture. Greet them, ask at least three past-simple questions about where they grew up, the traditions their family celebrated, and how they learned English. Use at least three new words from this lesson, and thank them at the end.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1Where did you grow up?Onde você cresceu?
2What traditions are important to you?Quais tradições são importantes para você?
3Did you find it hard to live abroad?Você achou difícil morar no exterior?
4How did you learn English?Como você aprendeu inglês?
5Thank you for sharing your culture with me.Obrigada por compartilhar a sua cultura comigo.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Mexico City -- Pre-trip Immersion

Planning a trip and talking about it with the past simple and the present perfect (I visited it last year vs I have already visited it). New vocabulary: itinerary, neighborhood, sightseeing, bustling, mural, vendor, ancient, ruins, crowded, affordable, stroll, highlight.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Itinerary -- a detailed plan or route for a trip
"My itinerary has one city for each day." (Roteiro)
Neighborhood -- a district or small area of a city
"I booked a hotel in a quiet neighborhood." (Bairro)
Sightseeing -- visiting interesting places as a tourist
"We spent the morning sightseeing downtown." (Passeio turístico)
Bustling -- full of energy, noise, and busy activity
"The market was bustling with people." (Movimentado)
Mural -- a large painting made on a wall
"The street murals tell the city's history." (Mural)
Vendor -- a person who sells things, often on the street
"A street vendor sold us fresh fruit." (Vendedor ambulante)
Ancient -- very old, from a distant past
"The ancient temple is over a thousand years old." (Antigo)
Ruins -- the remains of old buildings from the past
"We climbed the pyramids at the ruins." (Ruínas)
Crowded -- full of people; with little free space
"The historic center gets crowded on weekends." (Lotado)
Affordable -- not expensive; reasonable in price
"Street food in Mexico City is very affordable." (Acessível)
Stroll -- a slow, relaxed walk
"We took a stroll through the park." (Caminhada)
Highlight -- the best or most memorable part of something
"The pyramids were the highlight of the trip." (Destaque)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Itinerary
Neighborhood
Sightseeing
Bustling
Mural
Vendor
Ancient
Ruins
Crowded
Affordable
Stroll
Highlight

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca has always dreamed of visiting Mexico City. She has never traveled to Mexico before, so she planned her itinerary with care. Last week she booked a hotel in a quiet neighborhood and made a day-by-day plan for sightseeing. Her friend Daniel has been to the city three times. Last year he stayed in a bustling area and visited the ancient ruins. He told Tuca: "I have already seen the murals, and climbing the pyramids was the highlight of my trip." Remember: use the present perfect for experiences with no exact time ("I have visited the museum"), and the past simple for finished actions with a clear time ("I visited the museum last year").

1. Which sentence describes an experience with no exact time?
A "I have visited the ruins."
B "I visited the ruins last year."
C "I will visit the ruins tomorrow."
2. When do we use the past simple?
A for experiences with no exact time
B for finished actions with a clear past time
C for plans in the future
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I have seen the murals yesterday."
B "I saw the murals yesterday."
C "I have saw the murals yesterday."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Simple vs Present Perfect

GRAMMAR

Usado para falar de viagens e experiências. / Used to talk about trips and experiences.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
Present Perfecthave / has + particípio: experiência, SEM tempo exato / experience, no exact time"I have visited the ruins."
Past Simpleverbo no passado: ação terminada COM tempo claro / finished action, clear time"I visited the ruins last year."
Sinais do present perfectpalavras que pedem present perfect / time wordsever, never, already, yet, just
Sinais do past simplepalavras com tempo definido / finished-time wordsyesterday, last year, in 2019, ago

Key point / Ponto-chave: Se a frase tem um tempo exato (yesterday, last year), use o past simple. Se é uma experiência sem tempo definido, use o present perfect. Nunca junte os dois: "I have visited it last year" está errado.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have never to Mexico before."
"Last year Daniel in a bustling neighborhood."
"My has one city for each day."
"I have booked my hotel."
"The historic center gets very on weekends."
"Climbing the pyramids was the of the trip."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?She booked a small hotel in a quiet neighborhood.
?Tuca dreamed of visiting Mexico City for years.
?She added a slow stroll through the affordable markets.
?She called her friend Daniel, who has traveled there many times.
?She made an itinerary with sightseeing and the ancient ruins.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Have you ever been to Mexico City?
Você já esteve na Cidade do México?
I have always wanted to visit the ancient ruins.
Eu sempre quis visitar as ruínas antigas.
Last year I planned my whole itinerary.
Ano passado eu planejei todo o meu roteiro.
The murals were the highlight of my trip.
Os murais foram o destaque da minha viagem.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each travel situation.

You want to ask a friend about a travel experience, with no exact time. Which is correct?
A "Did you go to Mexico City?"
B "Have you ever been to Mexico City?"
C "Do you went to Mexico City?"
You are talking about a finished action with a clear time. Which is correct?
A "I have visited the ruins last year."
B "I visited the ruins last year."
C "I am visiting the ruins last year."
A friend asks what you have done so far to prepare your trip. Which is correct?
A "I have already booked my hotel."
B "I have book my hotel."
C "I has already book my hotel."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are planning your own trip to Mexico City. Describe your itinerary: say which neighborhood you have already chosen and one thing you have always wanted to do. Then talk about a real trip you took in the past (use the past simple with a clear time). Use at least four new words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Have you ever been to Mexico City?Você já esteve na Cidade do México?
2I have already planned my itinerary.Eu já planejei o meu roteiro.
3Last year I visited the ancient ruins.Ano passado eu visitei as ruínas antigas.
4What was the highlight of your trip?Qual foi o destaque da sua viagem?
5I have always wanted to see the murals.Eu sempre quis ver os murais.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

On the Trail -- Mont Blanc Preparation

Preparing for a mountain trek and talking about the future with will and going to (I will help you vs I am going to climb). New vocabulary: trail, backpack, gear, boots, layer, waterproof, altitude, summit, slope, forecast, shelter, first-aid kit.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Trail -- a path you follow through nature or mountains
"The trail to the summit takes six hours." (Trilha)
Backpack -- a bag you carry on your back for your things
"My backpack is full of water and snacks." (Mochila)
Gear -- the equipment you need for an activity
"Good gear keeps you safe on the mountain." (Equipamento)
Boots -- strong shoes for walking on rough ground
"My boots are waterproof and very strong." (Botas)
Layer -- a piece of clothing worn over or under another
"On cold days I wear three warm layers." (Camada de roupa)
Waterproof -- able to keep water out; not letting rain in
"A waterproof jacket keeps you dry." (Impermeável)
Altitude -- the height of a place above sea level
"At high altitude the air is thin and cold." (Altitude)
Summit -- the highest point of a mountain
"We reached the summit just before noon." (Cume)
Slope -- the sloping side of a hill or mountain
"The slope was steep, so we walked slowly." (Encosta)
Forecast -- a report of what the weather will be like
"The forecast says it will snow tonight." (Previsão do tempo)
Shelter -- a place that protects you from bad weather
"We found shelter under a big rock." (Abrigo)
First-aid kit -- a box with supplies to treat small injuries
"The guide always carries a first-aid kit." (Kit de primeiros socorros)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Trail
Backpack
Gear
Boots
Layer
Waterproof
Altitude
Summit
Slope
Forecast
Shelter
First-aid kit

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca is going to trek Mont Blanc next summer. She already made the plan, so she uses going to for her firm decision: "I am going to climb the summit." Her guide Marco checks the forecast every morning. One day he sees dark clouds and says, "Look, it is going to rain" -- a prediction from evidence he can see now. On the trail, Tuca finds a heavy backpack, and Marco says, "I will carry it for you" -- a quick decision at that moment. When Tuca asks about the summit, Marco gives his opinion: "I think we will reach the top." Remember: use going to for plans and evidence, and will for quick decisions, offers, and opinions.

1. Which sentence is a plan decided before now?
A "I am going to climb the summit."
B "I will carry it for you."
C "I think we will reach the top."
2. When do we use will?
A for a plan we made last week
B for a quick decision, offer, or opinion
C for a finished action in the past
3. You see dark clouds now. Which is correct?
A "It will rain."
B "It is going to rain."
C "It rains."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- will vs going to

GRAMMAR

Usado para falar do futuro: planos, decisões e previsões. / Used to talk about the future: plans, decisions, and predictions.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
going toam/is/are going to + verbo: plano decidido antes / a plan decided before now"I am going to climb the summit."
going toprevisão com evidência agora / prediction from present evidence"The sky is dark; it is going to storm."
willwill + verbo: decisão na hora, oferta, promessa / quick decision, offer, promise"I will carry the backpack for you."
willopinião ou previsão / opinion or prediction (I think, probably)"I think it will be cold."

Key point / Ponto-chave: Se você já planejou ou vê a evidência agora, use going to. Se decide na hora, oferece ajuda ou dá uma opinião, use will. Após will nunca use "to": "I will carry" (não "I will to carry").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am to climb the summit next summer."
"That bag is heavy. I carry your backpack."
"Look at the clouds. It is to rain."
"I think the trail be hard."
"She is going to buy new ."
"We will check the weather every morning."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?She bought strong waterproof boots and warm layers.
?Tuca decided to join the Mont Blanc trek.
?She checked the weather forecast for the whole week.
?She called Marco, an experienced mountain guide.
?She packed everything carefully in her new backpack.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

What gear am I going to need?
De que equipamento eu vou precisar?
I am going to buy waterproof boots.
Eu vou comprar botas impermeáveis.
If a storm comes, we will find shelter.
Se vier uma tempestade, vamos achar abrigo.
I think we will reach the summit.
Acho que vamos chegar ao cume.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each trek situation.

You have already planned to climb next summer. Which is correct?
A "I am going to climb Mont Blanc next summer."
B "I will climb Mont Blanc, I just decided now."
C "I climb Mont Blanc next summer."
Your friend's backpack is very heavy. What do you say?
A "I am going to carry it." (it was not planned)
B "I will carry it for you."
C "I will to carry it for you."
You see black clouds over the summit. Which is correct?
A "It will rains soon."
B "It is going to rain soon."
C "It going to rain soon."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are getting ready for the Mont Blanc trek. Say what gear you are going to pack and why (use going to). Then make two predictions about the trek: what you think the weather will be like, and what you will do if a storm comes (use will). Use at least four new words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1I am going to trek Mont Blanc next summer.Eu vou fazer o trekking do Mont Blanc no próximo verão.
2What gear am I going to need?De que equipamento eu vou precisar?
3If a storm comes, we will find shelter.Se vier uma tempestade, vamos achar abrigo.
4I think it will be cold at the summit.Acho que vai estar frio no cume.
5I will carry the heavy backpack for you.Eu vou carregar a mochila pesada para você.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

The Story So Far -- Progress Check

Look back at your English journey and review all the main structures: present simple, past simple, present perfect, and the future with will and going to. New vocabulary: progress, milestone, confident, fluent, achievement, improve, goal, challenge, proud, comfort zone, mistake, review.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Progress -- the process of getting better over time
"I can see my progress in every class." (Progresso)
Milestone -- an important point in a journey
"Lesson 10 is a big milestone for me." (Marco / etapa importante)
Confident -- sure of yourself; not afraid to try
"I feel confident when I speak English now." (Confiante)
Fluent -- able to speak a language smoothly and easily
"My dream is to become fluent in English." (Fluente)
Achievement -- something good you worked hard to do
"Speaking for ten minutes was a real achievement." (Conquista)
Improve -- to get better at something
"I practice every day to improve my English." (Melhorar)
Goal -- something you want to achieve in the future
"My goal is to travel and speak English everywhere." (Meta / objetivo)
Challenge -- something difficult that tests you
"Speaking on the phone is still a challenge for me." (Desafio)
Proud -- feeling happy about something you did well
"I am proud of how much I have learned." (Orgulhoso(a))
Comfort zone -- the situations where you feel safe and relaxed
"Speaking English pushes me out of my comfort zone." (Zona de conforto)
Mistake -- something you do wrong
"I am not afraid to make a mistake now." (Erro)
Review -- to look at something again to remember it
"Today we review the whole story so far." (Revisar / revisão)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Progress
Milestone
Confident
Fluent
Achievement
Improve
Goal
Challenge
Proud
Comfort zone
Mistake
Review

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca lives on a coffee farm in the mountains -- this is a fact, so she uses the present simple. Ten lessons ago, she started English and she was very nervous -- a finished time in the past, so the past simple. Since then, she has learned hundreds of words and she has visited many places in her lessons -- experiences up to now, so the present perfect. Today she is going to re-record her introduction, because she planned it before -- a plan, so going to. She thinks she will become fluent one day -- an opinion about the future, so will. One short story, four different tenses.

1. "Tuca lives on a coffee farm." Which tense is this?
A Present simple -- a fact or routine
B Past simple -- a finished action
C Future -- a plan
2. "She has learned hundreds of words." Which tense is this?
A Past simple
B Present perfect -- experience up to now
C Present simple
3. "She started English ten lessons ago." Which tense is this?
A Present perfect
B Future with going to
C Past simple -- a finished time in the past

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Four Tenses Review

GRAMMAR

Revisão das quatro estruturas principais do seu curso. / A review of the four main structures from your course.

Tense / TempoUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
Present simplefatos e rotinas / facts and routines"I live on a coffee farm."
Past simpleação terminada num tempo passado / a finished action in a past time"I started English in January."
Present perfectexperiência até agora (ever/never/already) / experience up to now"I have visited many places."
Future (will / going to)planos, decisões e previsões / plans, decisions, and predictions"I am going to practice. I will be fluent."

Key point / Ponto-chave: escolha o tempo pela ideia: fato (present simple), passado terminado (past simple), experiência até agora (present perfect), ou futuro (will / going to). A mesma história pode usar os quatro.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I on a coffee farm every day."
"I English ten lessons ago."
"I have hundreds of new words."
"Today I am to re-record my introduction."
"I think I become fluent one day."
"I am very of my progress."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the journey in the correct order.

?She found her way around a busy city and ordered food.
?Tuca learned to travel through the airport with confidence.
?She reached lesson ten and felt proud of her progress.
?She practiced talking about her life experiences.
?She made small talk and explored new cultures on her trips.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I am proud of my progress.
Tenho orgulho do meu progresso.
My goal is to become fluent in English.
Minha meta é me tornar fluente em inglês.
I have improved a lot since lesson one.
Eu melhorei muito desde a aula um.
Speaking on the phone is still a challenge for me.
Falar ao telefone ainda é um desafio para mim.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A friend asks how your English is going. Which sentence shows your progress over time?
A "I have improved a lot since I started."
B "I am improve a lot."
C "I improve yesterday."
You want to talk about a firm plan you already made for next month. Which is correct?
A "I practice every day next month."
B "I am going to practice every day next month."
C "I have practiced every day next month."
You want to describe a fact about your daily life. Which is correct?
A "I lived on a coffee farm." (it is still true now)
B "I live on a coffee farm."
C "I will live on a coffee farm." (it is true now)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Tell the story of your English so far. Say where you started and one mistake you used to make (past simple), what you have learned and how you have improved (present perfect), and what your next goal is (future with will or going to). Use at least four new words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1I have learned a lot in ten lessons.Eu aprendi muito em dez aulas.
2I feel more confident when I speak now.Me sinto mais confiante ao falar agora.
3My goal is to become fluent in English.Minha meta é me tornar fluente em inglês.
4I am proud of my progress.Tenho orgulho do meu progresso.
5I am not afraid to make a mistake.Não tenho medo de cometer um erro.
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Getting Around -- Public Transport

Learn to use the subway and the bus across a big city, and use the modal verbs should, could, and might to give advice, offer options, and talk about possibilities. New vocabulary: platform, fare, subway, schedule, transfer, line, rush hour, ticket machine, get on, get off.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Platform -- the flat area beside the track where you wait for a train
"Your train leaves from platform two." (Plataforma)
Fare -- the price you pay to travel by bus, train, or taxi
"A single fare costs two dollars." (Tarifa / passagem)
Subway -- an underground train system in a big city
"The subway is faster than a taxi at rush hour." (Metrô)
Schedule -- a list of the times when buses or trains arrive and leave
"Check the schedule before you leave home." (Horário)
Transfer -- to change from one bus or train line to another
"You transfer to the red line at the central station." (Fazer baldeação / trocar de linha)
Line -- a subway or bus service, often named by a color or number
"Take the green line and ride four stops." (Linha)
Rush hour -- the busy time when many people travel to or from work
"The platform is very crowded during rush hour." (Horário de pico)
Ticket machine -- a machine where you buy a ticket with coins or a card
"You can buy a ticket at the ticket machine." (Máquina de bilhetes)
Get on -- to enter a bus, train, or plane
"Get on the green line and ride four stops." (Entrar / embarcar)
Get off -- to leave a bus, train, or plane
"Get off at the market station and meet me at the exit." (Sair / desembarcar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Platform
Fare
Subway
Schedule
Transfer
Line
Rush hour
Ticket machine
Get on
Get off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca arrives in a new city and wants to reach the market. A local passenger gives her some advice: "You should buy a ticket at the ticket machine first." Advice uses should. Then he offers her two choices: "You could take the subway, or you could take the bus." When you talk about options you can choose, you use could. Finally, he warns her about something that is not certain: "The platform might be crowded at rush hour." For something that is only possible, you use might. Notice that after should, could, and might, the verb has no to: should buy, could take, might be.

1. "You should buy a ticket first." Why does he use should?
A To give advice -- the best thing to do
B To describe something only possible
C To talk about the past
2. "You could take the subway or the bus." What does could show here?
A A strong order
B Two options you can choose
C Something that already happened
3. "The platform might be crowded." What does might mean?
A It is certain and always true
B It is possible, but not sure
C It is the best advice

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Should / Could / Might

GRAMMAR

Três verbos modais para dar conselhos, oferecer opções e falar de possibilidades. / Three modal verbs for advice, options, and possibilities.

ModalUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
shouldconselho, a melhor ideia / advice, the best idea"You should buy a ticket first."
couldopção ou possibilidade que você pode escolher / an option you can choose"You could take the bus or the subway."
mightalgo possível, talvez, sem certeza / something possible, maybe"The train might be late."

Key point / Ponto-chave: após should, could e might, use o verbo na forma base, sem to: should buy, could take, might be. Negativo de conselho: should not (shouldn't). / After these modals, use the base verb with no to.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"You buy a ticket before you get on."
"You take the bus or the subway."
"The platform be crowded at rush hour."
"You to the red line at the central station."
"Get off at the market station and meet me at the ."
"You can buy a ticket at the ticket ."

Stage 2: Put the Journey in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the subway trip in the correct order.

?She found the right platform and waited for her train.
?Tuca walked to the station on the corner.
?She got off at the market station and met her friend at the exit.
?She bought a ticket at the machine and checked the fare.
?She got on the green line and rode four stops to the center.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Which train should I take to the market?
Qual trem eu devo pegar para o mercado?
You could take the subway or the bus.
Você poderia pegar o metrô ou o ônibus.
The platform might be crowded at rush hour.
A plataforma pode estar lotada no horário de pico.
I need to transfer to another line.
Preciso trocar para outra linha.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A friend does not know how to pay for the subway. Which sentence gives good advice?
A "You should buy a ticket at the ticket machine."
B "You should to buy a ticket."
C "You buy a ticket maybe."
You want to offer someone two different ways to travel. Which is correct?
A "You could takes the bus or the subway."
B "You could take the bus or the subway."
C "You could to take the bus."
You are not sure if the train will be late. Which sentence shows it is only possible?
A "The train must be late." (it is certain)
B "The train might be late."
C "The train might to be late."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A visitor wants to cross your nearest city by public transport. Give them advice with should, offer two options with could, and warn about one possible problem with might. Use at least four new words from this lesson, such as subway, fare, platform, transfer, and get off.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Which train should I take to the market?Qual trem eu devo pegar para o mercado?
2Where can I buy a ticket?Onde posso comprar um bilhete?
3Do I need to transfer to another line?Preciso trocar para outra linha?
4Which stop should I get off at?Em qual parada devo descer?
5How often do the trains run?Com que frequência os trens passam?
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Emergency! -- Health & Safety Abroad

Learn to ask for help when you feel sick far from home, describe your symptoms at a pharmacy, and use the first conditional (if + present, will) plus must and have to for strong rules. New vocabulary: pharmacy, fever, headache, sore throat, medicine, painkiller, symptom, pharmacist, emergency, dizzy.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Pharmacy -- a shop where you buy medicine and ask for health advice
"There is a pharmacy two blocks from the hotel." (Farmácia)
Fever -- when your body temperature is too high because you are ill
"I have a fever and I feel very tired." (Febre)
Headache -- a pain in your head that makes it hard to think
"I have a headache, so I need to rest." (Dor de cabeça)
Sore throat -- when the inside of your neck hurts, often before a cold
"I have a sore throat and I cannot talk well." (Dor de garganta)
Medicine -- a pill or liquid you take to treat an illness
"Take this medicine twice a day after meals." (Remédio)
Painkiller -- a pill that stops or reduces pain, like a headache
"Can I have a painkiller for my headache?" (Analgésico)
Symptom -- a sign in your body that shows you are ill, like a fever
"A fever and a cough are common symptoms of a cold." (Sintoma)
Pharmacist -- the person who works at a pharmacy and gives medicine
"The pharmacist gave me some medicine for my fever." (Farmacêutico)
Emergency -- a serious problem that needs help right now
"In an emergency, call one, one, two." (Emergência)
Dizzy -- when your head spins and you feel like you may fall
"I feel dizzy, so I should sit down." (Tonto / com tontura)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Pharmacy
Fever
Headache
Sore throat
Medicine
Painkiller
Symptom
Pharmacist
Emergency
Dizzy

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca wakes up with a fever in a new city. She makes a simple plan. "If I have a fever, I will rest at the hotel." This is the first conditional: if + present simple, will + base verb, for a real future possibility and its result. Later, the hotel front desk reminds her of a strong safety rule: "In an emergency, you must call one, one, two." We use must for a strong rule, often about safety. At the pharmacy, the pharmacist says: "You have to take this medicine after meals." We use have to for an obligation, often an outside rule. Notice that in the if part we use the present, not will: "if it rains", not "if it will rain".

1. "If I have a fever, I will rest." Which part is the condition?
A "If I have a fever" -- the condition
B "I will rest" -- the condition
C There is no condition
2. "You must call one, one, two." What does must show here?
A A soft suggestion you can ignore
B A strong rule, often about safety
C Something that happened in the past
3. In the if part, which form do we use?
A will + base verb ("if it will rain")
B the present simple ("if it rains")
C the past simple ("if it rained")

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- First Conditional + Must / Have to

GRAMMAR

Como falar de uma possibilidade real no futuro e dar regras fortes. / How to talk about a real future possibility and give strong rules.

StructureUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
If + present, will + basepossibilidade real no futuro e seu resultado / a real future possibility and its result"If I feel sick, I will rest."
must + baseregra forte, muitas vezes de segurança / a strong rule, often about safety"You must call the emergency number."
have to + baseobrigação, geralmente uma regra externa / an obligation, often an outside rule"You have to show your passport."

Key point / Ponto-chave: na parte do if, use o presente, não will: "if it rains", não "if it will rain". Após must e have to, use o verbo na forma base: must call, have to show. / In the if part, use the present simple; the will goes only in the result.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If I a fever, I will rest at the hotel."
"If the medicine does not help, I see a doctor."
"In an emergency, you call the emergency number."
"I have a fever and a sore ."
"Can I have a for my headache?"
"You take this medicine after meals."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put Tuca's sick day in the correct order.

?She walked to the pharmacy on the avenue.
?Tuca woke up with a headache and a sore throat.
?She went back to the hotel to rest and drink water.
?She called the front desk and asked for help.
?She described her symptoms and bought some medicine.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I have a fever and a sore throat.
Estou com febre e dor de garganta.
If I have a fever, I will rest.
Se eu estiver com febre, vou descansar.
In an emergency, you must call for help.
Em uma emergência, você precisa ligar pedindo ajuda.
Do I have to take it after meals?
Preciso tomar depois das refeições?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to make a plan for a real future possibility. Which is correct?
A "If I feel sick, I will rest at the hotel."
B "If I will feel sick, I will rest."
C "If I feel sick, I rest maybe."
You want to give a strong safety rule. Which is correct?
A "You must to call the emergency number."
B "You must call the emergency number."
C "You must called the emergency number."
You ask the pharmacist about a rule for the medicine. Which is correct?
A "Do I have to taking it after meals?"
B "Do I have to take it after meals?"
C "Do I have to took it after meals?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Make a short safety plan for your next trip. Say what you will do if you feel sick (if + will), what you must always carry (must), and what you have to do in a real emergency (have to). Use at least four new words from this lesson, such as pharmacy, fever, painkiller, symptom, and emergency.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1I have a fever and a sore throat.Estou com febre e dor de garganta.
2Where is the nearest pharmacy?Onde fica a farmácia mais próxima?
3Do I have to take it after meals?Preciso tomar depois das refeições?
4If it gets worse, I will see a doctor.Se piorar, vou consultar um médico.
5This is an emergency. Please call for help.Isto é uma emergência. Por favor, peça ajuda.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Money Matters -- Banks, Cash & Cards

Learn to change money, withdraw cash, and handle a bank abroad, and use the second conditional (if + past, would) for imaginary situations. New vocabulary: bank, account, cash, withdraw, exchange rate, currency, ATM, fee, receipt, balance.
0%
▼
