Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Bank -- a place that keeps your money safe and helps you with it
"I went to the bank to change some money." (Banco)
Account -- your personal record at a bank where your money is kept
"The money went straight into my bank account." (Conta)
Cash -- paper money and coins, not a card or a check
"Some small shops only take cash, not cards." (Dinheiro / espécie)
Withdraw -- to take money out of your bank account
"I need to withdraw two hundred dollars in cash." (Sacar)
Exchange rate -- how much one currency is worth in another currency
"The exchange rate today is five reais to one dollar." (Taxa de câmbio)
Currency -- the type of money a country uses, like the dollar or the euro
"The dollar is the currency of the United States." (Moeda)
ATM -- a cash machine where you take out money with your card
"There is an ATM in the airport hall." (Caixa eletrônico)
Fee -- extra money you pay for a service, like changing money
"The bank charges a small fee for every exchange." (Taxa / tarifa)
Receipt -- a paper or message that shows what you paid and when
"Keep your receipt for every transaction." (Recibo / comprovante)
Balance -- the amount of money you have in your account right now
"I checked my balance online before I paid." (Saldo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Bank
Account
Cash
Withdraw
Exchange rate
Currency
ATM
Fee
Receipt
Balance

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca is planning a trip and thinking about money. She dreams a little: "If I had more time and money, I would travel to Europe." This is the second conditional: if + past simple, would + base verb, for an imaginary or unlikely situation. The past here does not mean past time -- it shows the idea is imaginary. A friend gives advice: "If I were you, I would use a card and carry only a little cash." With the verb to be, we often use were for all persons. Compare with last class: the first conditional (if + present, will) is about a real possibility -- "If I have cash, I will pay now" -- while the second conditional is about a dream or a what-if. Be careful: in the if part, use the past, not would: "if I had", not "if I would have".

1. "If I had more money, I would travel." Is this real or imaginary now?
A Real -- she has the money now
B Imaginary -- she does not have the money now
C It happened in the past
2. "If I were you, I would use a card." What does this express?
A Polite advice in an imaginary case
B A real plan for tonight
C Something that already happened
3. In the if part of the second conditional, which form do we use?
A would + base verb ("if I would have")
B the past simple ("if I had")
C the present simple ("if I have")

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Second Conditional (If + Past, Would)

GRAMMAR

Como falar de situações imaginárias e dar conselhos educados. / How to talk about imaginary situations and give polite advice.

StructureUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
If + past, would + basesituação imaginária ou pouco provável agora / an imaginary or unlikely situation now"If I had more time, I would travel."
If I were you, would + baseconselho educado em caso imaginário / polite advice in an imaginary case"If I were you, I would use a card."
Compare: first conditionalpossibilidade real (aula passada) / a real possibility (last class)"If I have cash, I will pay."

Key point / Ponto-chave: o passado na parte do if não é tempo passado -- mostra que a situação é imaginária: "if I had", não "if I would have". Após would, use o verbo na forma base: would travel. Com to be, use were para todas as pessoas: "if I were you". / The past in the if part signals an imaginary idea, not past time.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If I more money, I would travel every year."
"If I lost my card, I call the bank at once."
"If I you, I would use a card abroad."
"I would like to two hundred dollars in cash."
"There is a small for every exchange."
"I checked my online before I paid."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put Tuca's bank visit in the correct order.

?She asked the teller to withdraw two hundred dollars in cash.
?Tuca went to the bank to change some money for her trip.
?She checked her account balance online to make sure everything was correct.
?She checked the exchange rate and the fee on the screen.
?She took her cash and kept the receipt in her bag.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I would like to change some money, please.
Eu gostaria de trocar um pouco de dinheiro, por favor.
What is the exchange rate today?
Qual é a taxa de câmbio hoje?
If I had more money, I would travel more.
Se eu tivesse mais dinheiro, eu viajaria mais.
If I were you, I would keep the receipt.
Se eu fosse você, eu guardaria o recibo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to talk about an imaginary situation. Which is correct?
A "If I had more money, I would travel more."
B "If I would have more money, I would travel."
C "If I have more money, I travel more."
You want to give polite advice. Which is correct?
A "If I was you, I would using a card."
B "If I were you, I would use a card."
C "If I were you, I will use a card."
You ask the teller to take money out of your account. Which is correct?
A "I would like to withdrawing cash."
B "I would like to withdraw some cash."
C "I would like to withdrew some cash."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you had a free month and extra money. Describe your dream trip: where you would go (if + past, would), and what advice you would give a friend about money (if I were you). Use at least four new words from this lesson, such as bank, cash, exchange rate, fee, and balance.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1I would like to change some money, please.Eu gostaria de trocar um pouco de dinheiro, por favor.
2What is the exchange rate today?Qual é a taxa de câmbio hoje?
3Is there a fee for this?Tem alguma taxa por isso?
4Can I have a receipt, please?Pode me dar um recibo, por favor?
5If I lost my card, I would call the bank at once.Se eu perdesse meu cartão, eu ligaria para o banco imediatamente.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Making Plans -- Reservations & Bookings

Learn to make, confirm, and change a reservation, and use reported speech (said/told + backshift) to pass on what people said. New vocabulary: reservation, booking, confirm, cancel, availability, deposit, check-in time, voucher, refund, deadline.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Reservation -- an arrangement to keep a room, table, or seat for you
"I made a reservation for two nights at the hotel." (Reserva)
Booking -- the act of reserving something, like a room or a tour
"My booking is for the fifteenth of next month." (Reserva / agendamento)
Confirm -- to say that something is sure and will happen
"The hotel called to confirm my reservation." (Confirmar)
Cancel -- to stop a plan or booking so it does not happen
"I had to cancel my booking because my plans changed." (Cancelar)
Availability -- whether a room or seat is free for the dates you want
"I checked the availability before I booked." (Disponibilidade)
Deposit -- money you pay first to hold a booking
"I paid a small deposit to hold the room." (Sinal / depósito)
Check-in time -- the time you can arrive and get your room at a hotel
"The check-in time is three in the afternoon." (Horário de entrada)
Voucher -- a paper or message that proves your booking is paid
"Show your voucher at the front desk." (Comprovante / voucher)
Refund -- money given back to you when you cancel a booking
"I got a full refund because I cancelled early." (Reembolso)
Deadline -- the last day or time to do something, like to cancel
"The deadline to cancel is seven days before arrival." (Prazo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Reservation
Booking
Confirm
Cancel
Availability
Deposit
Check-in time
Voucher
Refund
Deadline

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tuca calls the hotel and then tells her friend the news. The receptionist said, "We have availability." Later, Tuca reports it: "The receptionist said that they had availability." This is reported speech: we tell someone what another person said, and the verb usually moves one step into the past. Present (have) becomes past (had); will becomes would; can becomes could. We use said with no person after it, but told + a person: "She told me that the room was free." For requests, we use asked + person + to + verb: "She asked me to confirm by Friday." Reported speech is useful when you pass on a message about a booking, a deposit, or a deadline.

1. The receptionist said, "We have availability." How do we report it?
A She said that they have availability.
B She said that they had availability.
C She said me they have availability.
2. Which verb needs a person directly after it?
A said ("She said me...")
B told ("She told me...")
C Neither needs a person
3. To report a request, "Confirm by Friday," we say:
A She asked me to confirm by Friday.
B She asked me that I confirm by Friday.
C She said me confirm by Friday.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech (Said / Told, Backshift)

GRAMMAR

Como contar o que outra pessoa disse. / How to tell someone what another person said.

Direct speechReported / IndiretoChange / Mudança
"We have a room."She said that they had a room.presente → passado / have → had
"I will send the voucher."He told me that he would send it.will → would; told + pessoa / + person
"Confirm by Friday."She asked me to confirm by Friday.pedido: asked + pessoa + to + verbo / request

Key point / Ponto-chave: em reported speech, o verbo geralmente recua um passo no tempo (backshift): have→had, will→would, can→could. Use said (sem pessoa) ou told + pessoa. Para pedidos: asked + pessoa + to + verbo. / The verb usually shifts one step back; use said (no person) or told + person; for requests, asked + person + to + verb.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The hotel that they had availability for our dates."
"She me that the deposit was fifty dollars."
"He said the voucher arrive by email."
"The receptionist asked me confirm the booking by Friday."
"They said we would get a full if we cancelled early."
"The agent said the to cancel was seven days before arrival."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put Tuca's booking steps in the correct order.

?She paid a small deposit to confirm the booking.
?Tuca went online to check the availability for her dates.
?She saved the cancellation deadline in her phone, just in case.
?She called the hotel and made a reservation for two nights.
?The hotel sent her a voucher by email with the check-in time.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I would like to make a reservation for two nights.
Eu gostaria de fazer uma reserva para duas noites.
Can you confirm the check-in time, please?
Você pode confirmar o horário de entrada, por favor?
The hotel said that they had a room for us.
O hotel disse que tinha um quarto para nós.
She asked me to confirm the booking by Friday.
Ela me pediu para confirmar a reserva até sexta.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You report what the hotel said about a room. Which is correct?
A "The hotel said that they had a free room."
B "The hotel said me they have a free room."
C "The hotel told that they had a free room."
You report a request to confirm by Friday. Which is correct?
A "She asked me that I confirm by Friday."
B "She asked me to confirm by Friday."
C "She said me to confirm by Friday."
You report that the agent said "I will send the voucher." Which is correct?
A "He told that he will send the voucher."
B "He told me that he would send the voucher."
C "He said me he will send the voucher."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you called a hotel to make a booking, and now you are telling a friend what they said. Report at least three things using said, told, and asked (for example: the check-in time, the deposit, and the cancellation deadline). Use at least four new words from this lesson, such as reservation, availability, voucher, and refund.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1I would like to make a reservation, please.Eu gostaria de fazer uma reserva, por favor.
2Do you have availability for these dates?Você tem disponibilidade para estas datas?
3Can you confirm my booking, please?Você pode confirmar minha reserva, por favor?
4What is the deadline to cancel?Qual é o prazo para cancelar?
5They said I would get a full refund.Eles disseram que eu receberia um reembolso total.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Eating Out -- Ordering & Polite Requests

Learn to order a meal at a restaurant and ask for things politely with Could you...? and Would you mind...? New vocabulary: menu, course, dish, order, well-done, allergic, vegetarian, takeout, complaint, service charge.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Menu -- the list of food and drinks a restaurant offers
"Could I see the menu, please?" (Cardápio)
Course -- one part of a meal, such as the starter, the main, or the dessert
"We had a lovely three-course dinner." (Prato / etapa da refeição)
Dish -- a prepared plate of food with a name on the menu
"The pasta is the most popular dish here." (Prato / iguaria)
Order -- to ask the waiter to bring you food or drink
"Are you ready to order?" (Pedir / fazer o pedido)
Well-done -- cooked for a long time, so it is not pink inside (for meat)
"I would like my steak well-done, please." (Bem passado)
Allergic -- having a body reaction to a food, like nuts, so you must avoid it
"I am allergic to nuts, so I ask the waiter." (Alérgico)
Vegetarian -- a person who does not eat meat; or food made without meat
"Is this dish vegetarian?" (Vegetariano)
Takeout -- food you buy at a restaurant to eat at home or elsewhere
"We got takeout and ate it at the farm." (Comida para viagem)
Complaint -- when you tell someone politely that something is wrong
"We had a small complaint because the soup was cold." (Reclamação)
Service charge -- an amount for service that the restaurant adds to your bill
"A ten percent service charge is included." (Taxa de serviço)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Menu
Course
Dish
Order
Well-done
Allergic
Vegetarian
Takeout
Complaint
Service charge

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At a restaurant, you ask for things in a polite way. Instead of "Bring the menu," you say "Could you bring the menu, please?" After Could you, use the base verb (bring, not bringing). Another polite form is Would you mind + verb-ing: "Would you mind checking if this dish is vegetarian?" To ask a question politely, use Could you tell me + the question with no inversion: "Could you tell me what the special is?" (not "what is the special"). To order, use I would like: "I would like the steak well-done." These polite requests work for any situation: ordering food, making a small complaint, or asking about the service charge.

1. Which polite request is correct?
A Could you bringing the menu?
B Could you bring the menu, please?
C Could you to bring the menu?
2. After "Would you mind", which form do we use?
A the base verb ("Would you mind check...")
B the verb + -ing ("Would you mind checking...")
C to + verb ("Would you mind to check...")
3. After "Could you tell me...", the question is:
A "...what the special is?" (no inversion)
B "...what is the special?"
C "...what does the special?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Polite Requests (Could you / Would you mind)

GRAMMAR

Como pedir coisas de forma educada. / How to ask for things politely.

Structure / EstruturaExampleNote / Nota
Could you + verbo baseCould you bring the menu?verbo base, sem -ing / base verb
Would you mind + verbo-ingWould you mind checking the dish?verbo + -ing
Could you tell me + perguntaCould you tell me what the special is?sem inversão / no inversion
I would like + substantivoI would like the steak well-done."I want" educado / polite "I want"

Key point / Ponto-chave: use Could you + verbo base, ou Would you mind + verbo-ing, para pedir com educação. Após Could you tell me, a pergunta não inverte: "what the special is", não "what is the special". Para pedir um prato, use I would like. / Use Could you + base verb, or Would you mind + verb-ing; after Could you tell me, no inversion; to order, use I would like.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could you the menu, please?"
"Would you mind if this dish is vegetarian?"
"Could you tell me what the soup of the day ?"
"I would the steak well-done, please."
"I am to nuts, so I always ask the waiter."
"Could you tell me if the is already included?"

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put Tuca's dinner steps in the correct order.

?She ordered her steak well-done and asked him to bring some water.
?Tuca read the menu and chose a course to start with.
?She asked for the bill and noticed a service charge was already included.
?She called the waiter over and asked if the pasta dish was vegetarian.
?When the food arrived, she had a small complaint because one dish was cold.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Could you bring the menu, please?
Você poderia trazer o cardápio, por favor?
Would you mind checking if this is vegetarian?
Você se importaria de verificar se isto é vegetariano?
I would like the steak well-done, please.
Eu gostaria do bife bem passado, por favor.
Could you tell me if the service charge is included?
Você poderia me dizer se a taxa de serviço está incluída?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want the waiter to bring some water. Which is most polite and correct?
A "Would you mind bring some water?"
B "Would you mind bringing some water?"
C "Bring me water."
You want to know what is in a dish. Which is correct?
A "Could you tell me what is in this dish?"
B "Could you tell me what is this dish in?"
C "Could you to tell me this dish?"
You want to order a vegetarian main course. Which is correct?
A "I would like to a vegetarian dish."
B "I would like the vegetarian pasta, please."
C "I would liking the vegetarian pasta."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you take a friend out for dinner. Order two courses for yourself, ask the waiter one polite question (for example, if a dish is vegetarian, or what today's special is), and ask if the service charge is included. Use Could you, Would you mind, and I would like, and at least four new words from this lesson, such as menu, dish, well-done, and complaint.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Could you bring the menu, please?Você poderia trazer o cardápio, por favor?
2I would like the steak well-done, please.Eu gostaria do bife bem passado, por favor.
3Would you mind checking if this is vegetarian?Você se importaria de verificar se isto é vegetariano?
4I am allergic to nuts. Could you check this dish?Sou alérgico a castanhas. Você poderia verificar este prato?
5Could you tell me if the service charge is included?Você poderia me dizer se a taxa de serviço está incluída?
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Out and About -- Shopping & Returns

Learn to shop for clothes, ask for sizes, try things on, and make a return with too and not enough. New vocabulary: size, fit, try on, fitting room, browse, aisle, cashier, discount, faulty, exchange.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Size -- the number or letter that shows how big a piece of clothing is
"Do you have this in a smaller size?" (Tamanho)
Fit -- to be the right size and shape for your body
"These boots fit perfectly." (Servir / caber)
Try on -- to put on clothes in the shop to see if they fit
"Can I try this on before I buy it?" (Experimentar / provar)
Fitting room -- a small private room in a shop where you try clothes on
"The fitting rooms are at the back." (Provador)
Browse -- to look at things in a shop slowly, without deciding to buy yet
"I am just browsing, thank you." (Olhar / dar uma olhada)
Aisle -- the long, narrow space between rows of shelves in a shop
"Shoes are in the first aisle." (Corredor)
Cashier -- the person at the desk who takes your money and gives a receipt
"You pay at the cashier near the door." (Caixa / atendente do caixa)
Discount -- an amount taken off the normal price, so you pay less
"These boots have a thirty percent discount." (Desconto)
Faulty -- broken or not made correctly, so it does not work as it should
"The shirt was faulty; there was a hole in it." (Com defeito)
Exchange -- to give back an item and get a different one instead
"Can I exchange this for a bigger size?" (Trocar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Size
Fit
Try on
Fitting room
Browse
Aisle
Cashier
Discount
Faulty
Exchange

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

When you shop for clothes, you often say why something is wrong. To say that something is more than you want, use too before the adjective: "This jacket is too big." "These boots are too expensive." To say that something is less than you need, use not + adjective + enough: "This shirt is not big enough." Notice that too goes before the adjective, but enough goes after it. We never say "enough big" -- we say "big enough". If the size is wrong, you can ask the assistant for a different size, try it on in the fitting room, or, after you buy it, exchange it or ask for a refund if it is faulty.

1. Which sentence means the jacket is larger than you want?
A This jacket is too big.
B This jacket is not big enough.
C This jacket is enough big.
2. Where does "enough" go?
A before the adjective ("enough big")
B after the adjective ("big enough")
C after the noun ("big size enough")
3. "This shirt is not big enough" means the shirt is:
A too small for you
B too big for you
C the perfect size

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Too & Not Enough

GRAMMAR

Como dizer que algo é demais ou de menos. / How to say something is too much or not enough.

Structure / EstruturaExampleNote / Nota
too + adjetivoThis jacket is too big.mais do que o ideal / more than you want
too + adjetivoThese boots are too expensive."too" vem ANTES / before the adjective
not + adjetivo + enoughThis shirt is not big enough.menos do que o ideal / less than you need
not + adjetivo + enoughThe coat is not warm enough."enough" vem DEPOIS / after the adjective

Key point / Ponto-chave: too vem ANTES do adjetivo e significa "demais" (too big = grande demais). enough vem DEPOIS do adjetivo; not ... enough significa "de menos" (not big enough = pequeno demais). Diga "big enough", nunca "enough big". / "Too" goes before the adjective; "enough" goes after.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"This jacket is big for me."
"This shirt is not big ."
"Could you tell me where the is?"
"Do you have this jacket in a smaller ?"
"The shirt was , so I took it back."
"Can I this for a bigger size?"

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put Tuca's shopping trip steps in the correct order.

?She took it to the fitting room to try it on, but it was too big.
?Tuca walked into the store and started to browse the aisles.
?She remembered a faulty shirt, took it back with her receipt, and asked for a refund.
?She found a jacket she liked and asked the assistant if they had it in her size.
?The assistant brought a smaller size that was a perfect fit, and it had a discount.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Do you have this in a smaller size?
Você tem isto em um tamanho menor?
This jacket is too big for me.
Esta jaqueta é grande demais para mim.
Where can I try this on?
Onde posso experimentar isto?
This item is faulty. Could I have a refund?
Este item está com defeito. Eu poderia ter um reembolso?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

The shoes are smaller than you need. Which is correct?
A "These shoes are not enough big."
B "These shoes are not big enough."
C "These shoes are too enough big."
You want to test a jacket in the shop. Which is correct?
A "Can I try it on in the fitting room?"
B "Can I try on it in the fitting room?"
C "Can I on try it in the fitting room?"
The shirt you bought is faulty. Which is the best thing to say?
A "This shirt is too faulty enough."
B "This shirt is faulty. Could I exchange it or get a refund?"
C "This shirt browses faulty."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a shopping trip for clothes. Describe browsing the store, asking the assistant for your size, trying two things on, and saying if they fit using too or not enough. Then describe returning one faulty item and asking for an exchange or a refund. Use at least four new words from this lesson, such as size, fit, fitting room, and discount.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Do you have this in a smaller size?Você tem isto em um tamanho menor?
2This jacket is too big for me.Esta jaqueta é grande demais para mim.
3Where can I try this on?Onde posso experimentar isto?
4This item is faulty. Could I have a refund?Este item está com defeito. Eu poderia ter um reembolso?
5Can I exchange this for a different size?Posso trocar isto por um tamanho diferente?

The Dream Packing Words The Code Getting There Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Lesson 1 -- Travel & Social English

Tuca's
Journey Map

Day 1 -- Who Are You in English?

Chapter 1: The Dream

Let's Talk About You

"What is your favorite place you have ever visited?"
"What do you love most about traveling?"
"Where do you want to go next?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Where in the World
Have You Been?

On a piece of paper, write every country or city you have visited.
For each one, tell your teacher: when, who with, and one memory.

Chapter 1: The Dream

By the End of This Lesson

Introduce yourself confidently in English
Talk about your life, your farm, and your travels
Use present simple and past simple correctly
Chapter 2

Packing
Words

Essential vocabulary for your travels

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

a long trip from one place to another
Journey
a long trip from one place to another
"My journey to Morocco was unforgettable."
the place where someone is going
Destination
the place where someone is going or being sent
"Our destination was a small village in the mountains."
relating to a particular area
Local
relating to or found in a particular area
"I always try to eat local food when I travel."
the customs and way of life of a group
Culture
the customs, beliefs, art, and way of life of a group of people
"I love learning about the culture of new places."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

an exciting or unusual experience
Adventure
an exciting or unusual experience
"Traveling is always an adventure."
to travel to a place to learn about it
Explore
to travel to a place to learn about it
"I want to explore the streets of Tokyo."
the line that divides two countries
Border
the line that divides two countries or areas
"We crossed the border between Spain and Portugal."
the way you go from one place to another
Route
the way you go from one place to another
"What is the best route to the airport?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 9-10

something that happens to you
Experience
something that happens to you or something you do
"That was an amazing experience."
involving two or more countries
International
involving two or more countries
"She works for an international company."
0 / 2 words revealed
Vocabulary Drill

All 10 Words -- Your Turn

Use each word in a sentence about your life or your travels.

Journey Destination Local Culture Adventure Explore Border Route Experience International
Vocabulary Practice

Use the Words

1. Tell me about a trip that took a very long time.

"My journey to Europe took about twelve hours."

2. Where do you want to go on your next vacation?

"My next destination is Mexico City."

3. What kind of food do you eat when you travel?

"I always try local food. It is the best part of traveling."

4. What is the most exciting trip you have taken?

"Every trip is an adventure. I love the unexpected."

5. What do you like to do in a new city?

"I love to explore the streets and talk to people."

6. Have you ever crossed from one country into another by land?

"Yes, I crossed the border between Argentina and Chile."
Chapter 3

The Code

Present Simple & Past Simple

Grammar Discovery

Look at the Orange Words

"I travel to new places every year."
"I grow coffee in Sao Sebastiao da Grama."
"Last year I went to Mexico City."
"I visited the old market and tried local food."
Grammar Discovery

What Do They Have in Common?

The first two sentences describe habits and facts (now).
The last two describe completed actions (past).

TenseUseExample
Present SimpleHabits, routines, facts"I travel every year."
Present SimpleWhat you do / where you live"I grow coffee."
Past SimpleFinished actions (specific time)"I went to Mexico last year."
Past SimpleExperiences in the past"I visited the market."
Grammar Table

Present Simple vs Past Simple

Present SimplePast Simple
I travel every year.I traveled to Mexico last year.
She grows coffee.She grew up in Sao Paulo.
I live in a small town.I lived in Pocos de Caldas for 10 years.
I love learning new things.I loved the food in Morocco.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I go to Mexico last year."
"I went to Mexico last year."

With last year, yesterday, last month = always use Past Simple

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentences

I ___live in Sao Sebastiao da Grama. (now)
Last summer I ___explored the south of France.
She ___grows specialty coffee. (habit)
We ___visited the museum yesterday.
Chapter 4

Getting There

A conversation in Morocco

Context

A Hotel Lobby in Morocco

Tuca is checking in at a hotel in Marrakech.
She meets David, an American photographer, at the lobby.

Click "Next Line" to reveal the conversation one line at a time.

Dialogue

Tuca Meets David

D
"Hi! I am David. I am from California."
T
"Nice to meet you, David! I am Tuca, from Brazil."
D
"What brings you to Morocco?"
T
"I love exploring new cultures. This is my first time here."
D
"That sounds great! What do you do back home?"
T
"I have a coffee farm in Brazil. I grow specialty coffee."
D
"Wow, that is fascinating! I visited a coffee farm in Colombia last year."
T
"Really? I went to Colombia too! The coffee culture there is amazing."
D
"What is your favorite destination so far?"
T
"I think Japan. The culture is so different and beautiful."
D
"Japan is on my list! I want to explore Tokyo and Kyoto."
Dialogue

Final Lines

T
"You should! The local food is incredible."
D
"I definitely will. Nice talking to you, Tuca!"
Comprehension

About David

1. Where is David from?

David is from California, United States.

2. What is David's job?

He is a photographer. He travels a lot for work.

3. Which country did David visit last year?

He visited a coffee farm in Colombia last year.

Listening

Listen to Tuca

Tuca describes herself to a new friend at a hotel.

0:000:00
Speed

1. Where does Tuca live?

She lives in Sao Sebastiao da Grama, a small town in Sao Paulo state.

2. Where did Tuca go last year?

She went to Mexico City with her family.

3. Where does Tuca want to go next?

She wants to visit Japan and walk the Mont Blanc trail.

Listening 2

Airport Announcement

Listen to the announcement and answer the questions.

0:000:00
Speed

1. What is the flight number?

AA 1045

2. What is the destination?

Mexico City

3. What gate?

Gate 12

Chapter 5

More Practice

From guided to free

Oral Practice

Respond to Each Situation

1. Someone asks: "Do you like traveling?"

"Yes, I love traveling! I usually travel once or twice a year."

2. Tell me about your last trip.

"Last year I went to Mexico City with my family. We explored the local markets."

3. What is your job?

"I have a coffee farm in Brazil. I grow specialty coffee."

4. What is your favorite part of traveling?

"The most interesting thing is learning about different cultures."

5. Where do you want to go next?

"I want to explore Japan and walk the Mont Blanc trail."

6. Tell me something about your hometown.

"I live in Sao Sebastiao da Grama. It is a small town in Sao Paulo state."
Sentence Building

Unscramble the Sentences

year / I / every / travel / new / to / places

"I travel to new places every year."

went / Mexico / last / I / City / to / year

"Last year I went to Mexico City."

coffee / in / grow / I / a / town / small

"I grow coffee in a small town."

local / tried / the / we / food / and / amazing / it / was

"We tried the local food and it was amazing."

met / people / on / wonderful / I / my / trip / last

"I met wonderful people on my last trip."

Quick Fire

Quick Challenges

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
You meet a new person at a hotel. Introduce yourself: your name, where you are from, and what you do.
"Hi, I am Tuca. I am from Brazil. I have a coffee farm."
Spot the Error

Find the Mistakes

"I go to Mexico last year."

"I went to Mexico last year."

"She travel to Japan every summer."

"She travels to Japan every summer."

"Yesterday I explore the old city."

"Yesterday I explored the old city."

"I am live in Sao Paulo state."

"I live in Sao Paulo state."

0 / 4 errors found
Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free -- show what you learned

Guided Role-Play

Level 1: Guided

Hotel Lobby in Mexico City

You meet a traveler at a hotel in Mexico City. Introduce yourself and talk about your trip. Use the keywords below.

Tuca Brazil coffee farm journey explore local food culture adventure
Semi-Free Role-Play

Level 2: Less Support

Same scenario, less help

Same hotel lobby. A new traveler asks about your life. Tell them about yourself, your travels, and your plans. Only a few keywords this time.

61 years old specialty coffee Mexico City
Free Role-Play

Level 3: Free Conversation

No script. Just talk.

Full conversation with no support. You are at a hotel restaurant. A traveler sits next to you and starts talking. Be yourself. Talk about your life, your trips, your coffee, your family. No keywords.

Artifact

Your Boarding Pass

BOARDING PASS Alumni Airlines
Passenger
DIAS / TUCA
Flight
AA 1045
From
GRU - Sao Paulo
To
MEX - Mexico City
Gate
12
Seat
14A
Date
15 JUN 2026
Boarding
10:30 AM
Comprehension

Boarding Pass Questions

1. Where is Tuca flying from?

She is flying from Sao Paulo (GRU).

2. What is her seat number?

Her seat is 14A (a window seat).

3. What time does she need to board?

She needs to board at 10:30 AM at Gate 12.

Error Correction

Let's Fix Together

Wrap Up

What did we learn today?

Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself in English (name, age, city, job)
I can use present simple for habits and facts
I can use past simple for completed actions
I know 10 travel vocabulary words
I can talk about my last trip
I can talk about my coffee farm
I can have a simple conversation with a traveler
I can understand an airport announcement

Day 1 -- Complete

The Journey Begins

"The world is a book, and those who do not travel read only one page."

-- Saint Augustine

Lesson 2 -- Travel & Social English

Tuca's
Takeoff

Day 2 -- At the Airport

Chapter 1: The Dream

Your Last Trip

"What did you write about your last trip?"
"What was the most interesting thing you remembered?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

At the Airport

"Think about the last time you were at an airport. What was easy? What was difficult?"
"Have you ever missed a flight or had a problem at the airport?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

By the End of This Lesson

Know 10 airport vocabulary words
Use present continuous for NOW actions
Use "going to" for future plans
Check in for a flight and find your gate
Chapter 2

Packing Words

Airport vocabulary

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

a card that shows your seat number and flight details
Boarding pass
a card that shows your seat number and flight details
"Please have your boarding pass ready before you reach the gate."
the process of registering for your flight
Check-in
the process of registering for your flight at the airport
"The check-in counter closes 45 minutes before departure."
the area where passengers wait and board the plane
Gate
the area where passengers wait and board the plane
"All passengers for Flight AM 742, please proceed to Gate 22."
when a plane leaves the airport
Departure
when a plane leaves the airport
"The departure time has been changed to 11:15."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

bags and suitcases you take when traveling
Luggage
bags and suitcases you take when traveling
"You can pick up your luggage at the baggage claim area."
when something happens later than expected
Delay
when something happens later than expected
"There is a 30-minute delay due to weather conditions."
an official message spoken over a loudspeaker
Announcement
an official message spoken over a loudspeaker
"Listen to the announcement for your gate number."
a large building at the airport
Terminal
a large building at the airport for arrivals and departures
"Terminal 3 is for international flights."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 9-10

a small bag you take on the plane with you
Carry-on
a small bag you take on the plane with you
"Your carry-on must fit under the seat in front of you."
the checkpoint where officers check bags and passengers
Security
the checkpoint where officers check bags and passengers
"Please remove your laptop at the security checkpoint."
0 / 2 words revealed
Vocabulary Drill

Point and Say

The card you give the agent before getting on the plane.

Boarding pass

The area in the airport where you wait before getting on the plane.

Gate

When the flight leaves later than planned.

Delay

The small bag you take inside the plane.

Carry-on
Oral Practice

Use the Word

You need to register for your flight. What do you do?

"I need to check in for my flight."

A voice on the loudspeaker says something important. What is that?

"That is an announcement."

You have two big suitcases for your trip. What do you call them?

"That is my luggage."

Officers check your bags before you enter the airport area. What is this place?

"That is the security checkpoint."
Chapter 3

The Code

Present Continuous + Going to

Grammar Discovery

What Do You Notice?

"I am waiting at the gate."
"She is checking in right now."
"I am going to fly to Mexico City."
"They are going to board the plane."

What pattern do you see?

Grammar

Reveal the Rule

Present Continuous

Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing

For actions happening NOW

Going to

Subject + am/is/are + going to + verb

For future plans and intentions

Grammar Forms

Present Continuous & Going to

FormPresent ContinuousGoing to
+I am waiting at the gate.I am going to fly to Mexico.
-She is not checking luggage.He is not going to check luggage.
?Are you waiting?Are you going to fly?
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I am go to the airport now."
"I am going to the airport now."
"They going to miss the flight."
"They are going to miss the flight."

Remember: am/is/are is always needed!

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentences

I ___am checking in for my flight right now.
The plane ___is departing in one hour.
We ___are going to arrive at 3 PM tomorrow.
She ___is looking for her boarding pass right now.
Chapter 4

Getting There

At the check-in counter

Context

Airport Check-in

Tuca is at the airport, ready to fly to Mexico City.
She is at the check-in counter with Mark, the airline agent.

Click "Next Line" to reveal the conversation one line at a time.

Dialogue

Tuca Meets Mark

M
"Good morning! Where are you flying to today?"
T
"Good morning! I am going to Mexico City."
M
"Great! Can I see your passport and boarding pass, please?"
T
"Here you go. I checked in online last night."
M
"Perfect. Are you checking any luggage today?"
Dialogue

At the Counter

T
"Yes, I have one suitcase. And this is my carry-on."
M
"Your luggage is all set. Your flight is departing from Gate 22."
T
"Thank you! Is the flight on time?"
M
"There is a small delay. They are boarding at 11:15 instead of 10:45."
T
"No problem. I am going to wait at the gate. Thank you!"
Comprehension

About Mark

1. What did Mark ask Tuca to show him?

He asked for her passport and boarding pass.

2. What gate did Mark say the flight departs from?

He said the flight departs from Gate 22.

3. What news did Mark give about the flight time?

He said there is a small delay. Boarding is at 11:15 instead of 10:45.

Listening 1

Boarding Announcement

Listen to the airport announcement and answer the questions.

0:000:00
Speed

1. What is the flight number?

AM 742

2. What gate is the flight boarding at?

Gate 22

3. Which rows board first?

Rows 20 through 35

Listening 2

Gate Change

Listen carefully. Something has changed!

0:000:00
Speed

1. What changed?

The gate changed from Gate 22 to Gate 18.

2. What should passengers do?

They should proceed to Gate 18 immediately.

Comprehension

Compare the Announcements

1. What is the original gate? What is the new gate?

The original gate was 22. The new gate is 18.

2. If you are Tuca, what do you do now?

I go to Gate 18 immediately because boarding is in progress.

3. What do both announcements ask you to have ready?

Your boarding pass and identification.

Chapter 5

More Practice

From guided to free

Oral Practice

Respond to Each Situation

1. You are at the airport right now. Tell someone what you are doing.

"I am checking in for my flight to Mexico City."

2. Tell someone your plan for this afternoon.

"I am going to fly to Mexico City this afternoon."

3. Your friend asks about the flight. What is happening?

"There is a small delay. They are boarding at 11:15."

4. Ask someone where your gate is.

"Excuse me, where is Gate 22?"
Sentence Building

Unscramble the Sentences

am / I / at / waiting / gate / the

"I am waiting at the gate."

going / she / to / is / Mexico / fly / to / City

"She is going to fly to Mexico City."

checking / are / they / luggage / any / today / ?

"Are they checking any luggage today?"

boarding / the / from / flight / is / Gate / 22 / departing

"The flight is departing from Gate 22."

not / he / going / is / check / to / luggage

"He is not going to check luggage."

Quick Fire

Quick Challenges

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
You arrive at the airport. Go to the check-in counter and tell the agent where you are flying.
"Good morning! I am going to Mexico City."
Spot the Error

Find the Mistakes

"I am go to the airport now."

"I am going to the airport now."

"They going to miss the flight."

"They are going to miss the flight."

"She is check in for her flight."

"She is checking in for her flight."

"We is going to arrive late."

"We are going to arrive late."

0 / 4 errors found
Grammar Practice

Fill in the Blanks

Right now, I ___am looking for my gate. (look)
Tomorrow, we ___are going to visit the museum. (visit)
The passengers ___are boarding the plane now. (board)
She ___is going to buy a coffee before the flight. (buy)
Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free -- show what you learned

Guided Role-Play

Level 1: Guided

Airport Check-in Counter

You are checking in for your flight to Mexico City. The agent asks you questions. Use the keywords below to help you.

Mexico City boarding pass one suitcase carry-on Gate 22 delay wait going to
Semi-Free Role-Play

Level 2: Less Support

Same scenario, less help

Same check-in counter. The agent asks about your flight. Check in, ask about your gate, and handle a delay announcement. Only a few keywords this time.

online check-in 11:15 Gate 18
Free Role-Play

Level 3: Free Conversation

No script. Just navigate the airport.

You are at the airport. Your gate changed, you heard an announcement you did not understand, and you need help. Talk to the people around you. No keywords.

Updated Boarding Pass

Passenger
DIAS / TUCA
Flight
AM 742
From
GRU - Sao Paulo
To
MEX - Mexico City

18
22
Seat
8F
Date
20 JUN 2026
Boarding
11:15 AM
10:45 AM
Comprehension

Boarding Pass Questions

1. What was the original gate? What is the new gate?

The original gate was 22. The new gate is 18.

2. What was the original boarding time? What is the new time?

The original time was 10:45 AM. The new time is 11:15 AM.

3. What is Tuca's flight number and destination?

Flight AM 742 to Mexico City.

Wrap Up

What did we learn today?

Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I know 10 airport vocabulary words
I can use present continuous for actions happening now
I can use "going to" for future plans
I can check in for a flight
I can understand an airport announcement
I can ask about my gate and handle a gate change
I can talk about delays and boarding times
I can read and understand a boarding pass

Day 2 -- Complete

Ready for Takeoff

"Travel is the only thing you buy that makes you richer."

-- Anonymous

Lesson 3 -- Travel & Social English

Tuca's
Travel Mosaic

Day 3 -- Have You Ever?

Chapter 1: The Dream

From Airports to Experiences

Last lesson: navigating airports and departures.
Today: talking about your entire travel history.

What is one thing you remember from your airport homework?
Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Warm-Up

"How many countries have you visited in your life?"
"What is the most interesting food you have ever tried?"
"Have you ever met someone while traveling who became a friend?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

By the End of This Lesson

Use present perfect to talk about life experiences
Ask and answer "Have you ever...?" questions
Describe unforgettable travel encounters and connections
Chapter 2: Packing Words

New Vocabulary

8 words to describe your travel experiences

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Travel Experience Words (1-4)

the style of cooking from a region
Cuisine
the style of cooking from a particular country or region
"The cuisine in Thailand is spicy and diverse."
moving from place to place
Nomadic
moving from place to place instead of staying in one location
"Some people live a nomadic lifestyle, always moving."
showing great variety
Diverse
showing a great deal of variety; very different
"Brazil is a very diverse country with many cultures."
impossible to forget
Unforgettable
impossible to forget; very memorable
"Meeting that family was an unforgettable experience."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Travel Experience Words (5-8)

an unexpected meeting with someone
Encounter
an unexpected meeting with someone
"I had a wonderful encounter with a local artist."
extremely interesting and attractive
Fascinating
extremely interesting and attractive
"The history of that city is absolutely fascinating."
a feeling of understanding between people
Connection
a feeling of understanding between people
"I felt a deep connection with the people there."
not proud; simple and modest
Humble
not proud; simple and modest
"The family was humble but incredibly generous."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Word Check

1. "I love trying the local _____ wherever I go." (cuisine)
2. "The market was _____ — I could not stop looking." (fascinating)
3. "I made a beautiful _____ with a local family." (connection)
4. "That trip was completely _____." (unforgettable)
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Say It in Context

Repeat each sentence, then create your own.

"I had a humble meal with a local family."
"The encounter with that artist was unforgettable."
"Brazil has a very diverse food culture."
"Some communities still have a nomadic way of life."
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Words You Already Know

From Lesson 1 — use each one in a sentence about your travels.

journey
explore
destination
experience
culture
Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

The present perfect for life experiences

Chapter 3: The Code

What Do These Have in Common?

"I have visited about 20 countries."
"She has never been to Japan."
"Have you ever tried Thai food?"
"We have always wanted to go to Peru."

Look at the orange words. What pattern do you see?

Chapter 3: The Code

The Present Perfect

For life experiences — when the exact time does not matter.

FormStructureExample
+have/has + past participleI have visited 20 countries.
-have/has + not + past participleI have never been to Spain.
?Have/Has + subject + past participleHave you ever been to Asia?
Chapter 3: The Code

Trigger Words

These words SIGNAL the present perfect.

ever
questions
never
negative experiences
already
done before expected
yet
not done, expected
before
at some past point
so far
up to now
Chapter 3: The Code

Common Mistake

"I have been to Japan last year."
"I went to Japan last year." (specific time = past simple)
"I have been to Japan." (no specific time = present perfect)
Chapter 3: The Code

Fill In

I _____ never _____ to Africa. ?have ... been
_____ you ever _____ local cuisine in India? ?Have ... tried
She _____ already _____ 30 countries. ?has ... visited
We _____ not _____ Morocco yet. ?have ... visited
I _____ always _____ to try nomadic life. ?have ... wanted
Chapter 4: Getting There

In Context

Tuca meets Carlos at a cultural festival

Chapter 4: Getting There

The Scene

You are at a cultural festival.
You meet Carlos from Spain.
You talk about your travel experiences.

Chapter 4: Getting There

Dialogue (1/2)

C
Carlos: "This is a wonderful event. Have you been to one like this before?"
T
Tuca: "No, this is my first time. But I have always wanted to attend a cultural festival."
C
Carlos: "Where are you from? Have you traveled much?"
T
Tuca: "I am from Brazil. I have visited about 20 countries so far."
C
Carlos: "That is impressive! Have you ever been to Spain?"
Chapter 4: Getting There

Dialogue (2/2)

T
Tuca: "No, I have never been to Spain, but it is on my bucket list!"
C
Carlos: "You should visit! The cuisine is unforgettable. I have tried food from many countries, but Spanish food is special."
T
Tuca: "I have always been fascinated by diverse cultures. That is why I travel."
C
Carlos: "Have you ever had an encounter that changed your perspective?"
T
Tuca: "Yes! I once ended up staying three extra days in Morocco because I made such a wonderful connection with a local family."
Chapter 4: Getting There

About Carlos

1. Where is Carlos from?

Spain.

2. What does Carlos say about Spanish cuisine?

It is unforgettable. He has tried food from many countries, but Spanish food is special.

3. What question does Carlos ask about Tuca's travel history?

"Have you ever been to Spain?" and "Have you ever had an encounter that changed your perspective?"

Chapter 4: Getting There

Listening 1: Sofia's Travels

Listen carefully. How many countries has Sofia visited?

0:000:00
Chapter 4: Getting There

Listening 2: The Interview

A traveler talks about a surprising destination.

0:000:00
Chapter 4: Getting There

Did You Understand?

1. In Listening 1, where was Sofia's most fascinating encounter?

With a nomadic family in Mongolia.

2. In Listening 2, which country surprised the traveler?

Japan.

3. How long did the traveler in Listening 2 end up staying?

Two extra weeks.

Chapter 5: Practice

More Practice

Using the present perfect in real situations

Chapter 5: Practice

Respond with Present Perfect

1. Name a country you have visited.

2. Name a food you have never tried.

3. Name a place you have always wanted to visit.

4. Tell me about an unforgettable encounter you have had.

Chapter 5: Practice

Build the Sentence

Ihaveneverbeento Egypt
Answer: "I have never been to Egypt."
Haveyouevertriedlocal cuisine
Answer: "Have you ever tried local cuisine?"
Shehasalreadyvisited30 countries
Answer: "She has already visited 30 countries."
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Fire

Someone asks if you have visited Europe. You have been to France and Italy. What do you say?

"Yes, I have. I have been to France and Italy."

1 / 6
Chapter 5: Practice

Spot the Error

"I have went to Japan last year."

"I went to Japan last year." (specific time = past simple)

"Have you ever went to a cultural festival?"

"Have you ever been to a cultural festival?" (been, not went)

"She have visited 30 countries."

"She has visited 30 countries." (she = has, not have)

"I never have been to Africa."

"I have never been to Africa." (have + never + past participle)

Chapter 5: Practice

Complete with Present Perfect

I _____ always _____ fascinated by diverse cultures. ?have ... been
_____ you ever _____ an unforgettable encounter? ?Have ... had
We _____ never _____ local cuisine in Mongolia. ?have ... tried
She _____ already _____ a deep connection with the family. ?has ... made
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Your Turn

From guided to free — show what you have learned

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Guided

Situation: You are at a cultural festival. You meet a traveler. Talk about your travel experiences using present perfect.

Your role: Tuca (yourself)

20 countries Morocco never ... Spain bucket list diverse cultures
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Semi-Free

Situation: You meet a tourist at a coffee farm event. Tell them about your travel experiences. Ask about theirs.

Use: have you ever...? / I have never... / I have always wanted to...

coffee farm unforgettable connection fascinating
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Free

Free conversation: Talk about your 3 most unforgettable travel experiences. Your teacher will ask questions. Use present perfect naturally.

No keywords. Speak freely. Use everything you have learned today.

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Tuca's Travel Passport

Travel Experience Passport
Tuca Dias
Countries visited20 so far
Most unforgettableMorocco
Bucket listSpain, Japan
Best connectionFamily in Morocco
Travel styleCultural & diverse
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Passport Questions

1. How many countries has Tuca visited?

"She has visited 20 countries so far."

2. Has Tuca ever been to Spain?

"No, she has never been to Spain, but it is on her bucket list."

3. Where did Tuca make her best connection?

"She made her best connection with a family in Morocco."

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Error Correction

Errors from today's lesson. Can you fix them?

Type errors here during the lesson...

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Wrapping Up

What did you learn today?

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

What I Learned

  • I can use present perfect for life experiences
  • I can ask "Have you ever...?" questions
  • I know the difference between present perfect and past simple
  • I can use new vocabulary: cuisine, nomadic, diverse, unforgettable, encounter, fascinating, connection, humble
  • I can describe travel experiences and connections with people

Day 3 Complete!

Badge earned: Experiences

"Travel is the only thing you buy that makes you richer."

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

For Next Time

1
Write 10 "Have you ever...?" questions about travel experiences
2
Record a 2-minute audio about your 3 most unforgettable travel experiences (use present perfect!)
End of Lesson 3

See You Next Time!

"The world is a book and those who do not travel read only one page."

-- Saint Augustine

T
LESSON 1

Complementary Materials -- Lesson 1

Extra content to reinforce your learning outside of class.

Film
Eat Pray Love (2010)

A woman travels to Italy, India, and Indonesia to find herself. Themes of travel, self-discovery, and cultural immersion. Great travel vocabulary in context.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note 5 travel words you recognize from our lesson.

Podcast
Rick Steves' Europe

Travel stories told at a calm, clear pace. Perfect for B1 listening practice. Focus on European destinations with rich cultural descriptions.

Tip: listen to one episode and write down 3 new words you learned.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Somebody Feed Phil -- Season 1, Episode 1

Phil Rosenthal explores local food and culture around the world. Fun, light, with lots of travel and food vocabulary. Great for understanding casual American English.

Tip: watch the first 15 minutes and note how Phil introduces himself to locals.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Materials -- Lesson 2

Airport and travel-related content to reinforce Lesson 2 vocabulary and grammar.

Film
Up in the Air (2009)

George Clooney plays a man who travels constantly for work. Excellent airport vocabulary, check-in scenes, and real travel situations. Great for B1 listening practice.

Tip: watch the first 20 minutes with English subtitles. Write down every airport word you hear.

Podcast
6 Minute English -- BBC (Airports episode)

Short BBC podcast about airports. Clear British and international English at a comfortable pace for B1. Covers airport vocabulary and travel experiences.

Tip: listen twice. First time just for understanding. Second time, write down 3 new words.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
How to Navigate an Airport

Practical guide with real airport vocabulary. Shows check-in, security, gates, and boarding step by step. Great visual support for the words you learned in Lesson 2.

Tip: watch once with subtitles off, then again with subtitles on. Note how they say "boarding pass", "gate", and "carry-on".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Materials -- Lesson 3

Travel experiences, cultural encounters, and present perfect in action.

Film
The Bucket List (2007)

Two men create a list of things they want to do before they die. Full of travel experiences, cultural encounters, and unforgettable moments. Great for present perfect in context: "I have always wanted to..."

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note every time they use "have you ever" or "I have never".

Podcast
Travel Diaries -- BBC World Service

People share their most unforgettable travel stories. Diverse accents, fascinating encounters, and humble connections across cultures. Great listening practice for B1.

Tip: listen to one episode and write 3 sentences using "I have..." about the traveler's experiences.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
50 People Tell Us Their Favorite Travel Experience

Short, diverse answers from 50 people about their most unforgettable travel moments. Many use present perfect naturally: "I have been to...", "The best trip I have ever had was..." Great for listening and vocabulary.

Tip: watch the first 10 minutes and count how many times people use "have" + past participle.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 -- Street Smart: Asking for Directions

Series
Somebody Feed Phil -- "Mexico City"

Phil wanders Mexico City on foot, asking locals where to go and how to get there. Perfect for your upcoming trip: listen for how he asks for directions and how people answer with turns, corners, and landmarks.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down every direction phrase you hear (turn, go straight, next to, opposite). Then describe one route Phil takes using three new words.

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "Asking for and Giving Directions"

A short, slow lesson dedicated to street language: imperatives, prepositions of place, and the polite phrases you need to stop a stranger and find your way in any city.

Pause after each direction and repeat it aloud. Note three prepositions of place the hosts use, and compare them with how Mateo guided Tuca in today's dialogue.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Directions: How to find your way"

A clear, practical video on giving and following directions in English, with on-screen maps showing intersections, roundabouts, crosswalks, and landmarks. Directly useful for travelling alone.

After watching, record a 60-second route from your hotel to a place you love. Use at least four new words and three imperatives (Go, Turn, Cross).

Lesson 5 -- Table Talk: Ordering & Food Culture

Series
Taco Chronicles (Las Cronicas del Taco) -- "Pastor"

A Netflix documentary on the history and culture of Mexican tacos. Perfect before your trip to Mexico City: listen for how people describe flavors, ingredients, and the experience of ordering at a real taqueria.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down five food words you hear (appetizer, main course, spicy, flavor, side dish). Then describe one dish using "I would like..." as if you were ordering it.

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "At the Restaurant"

A short, slow lesson dedicated to restaurant language: "I would like", asking for recommendations, ordering a main course and a beverage, and asking for the bill politely.

Pause after each phrase and repeat it aloud. Note three polite ways to order with "would like", and compare them with how Tuca ordered in today's dialogue.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Ordering food in a restaurant"

A clear, practical video on ordering in English: how to ask for recommendations, choose an appetizer, a main course and a dessert, and how to handle the bill and the tip. Directly useful for travelling alone.

After watching, record a 60-second order at an imaginary Mexican restaurant. Use at least four new words and three sentences with "I would like".

Lesson 6 -- Small Talk: Starting Conversations

Series
Friends -- any scene at Central Perk

The classic sitcom is set largely in a coffee shop, where the characters constantly make small talk. Perfect for hearing natural icebreakers, follow-up questions, and casual chat in English.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three ways a character starts a conversation, and one question with "Have you ever...?" Then try saying them aloud.

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "Making Small Talk"

A short, slow lesson dedicated to conversation language: icebreakers, safe topics, asking "Have you ever...?", and keeping a chat going with follow-up questions.

Pause after each phrase and repeat it aloud. Note three icebreakers and compare them with how Tuca started her chat with Daniel in today's dialogue.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "How to make small talk"

A clear, practical video on starting conversations in English: choosing safe topics, using the present perfect to ask about experiences, and avoiding awkward silences. Directly useful for travelling alone.

After watching, record a 60-second small-talk conversation with an imaginary stranger. Use at least four new words and two questions with "Have you ever...?"

Lesson 7 -- Culture Curious: Asking Deep Questions

Series
Somebody Feed Phil -- any episode (Netflix)

Phil travels the world and is genuinely curious, constantly asking locals about their food, traditions, and background. A perfect model for friendly culture interviews and past-simple questions.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three questions Phil asks a local, and rewrite each one in the past simple with "did". Then say them aloud.

Podcast
Cultures & Traditions -- "Asking About Where You Are From"

A slow, clear lesson about talking across cultures: customs, heritage, etiquette, and how to ask respectful questions about a person's background and past.

Pause after each question and repeat it aloud. Note three culture words and compare them with the questions Tuca asked Kenji in today's interview.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Past simple questions"

A clear, practical video on forming questions about the past: question word, "did", subject, and the base verb. Directly useful for interviewing people and avoiding the most common mistakes.

After watching, record a 60-second culture interview with an imaginary foreigner. Use at least four new words and four past-simple questions.

Lesson 8 -- Mexico City: Pre-trip Immersion

Series / Documentary
Street Food: Latin America -- "Mexico City" (Netflix)

A beautiful episode about the bustling markets, street vendors, and affordable food of Mexico City. Perfect for hearing how locals describe their neighborhood and traditions.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two sentences in the present perfect (I have seen...) and two in the past simple (Last year they...) about what you watched.

Podcast
Zero To Travel -- "Planning the Perfect Trip"

A slow, clear episode about building an itinerary, choosing a neighborhood, and getting ready for sightseeing in a new city. Full of useful travel vocabulary.

Pause and repeat each travel phrase aloud. Note three new words and use each one to talk about your own Mexico City plan.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Present perfect vs past simple"

A clear, practical video on the difference between the two tenses: experiences with no exact time versus finished actions with a clear time. Exactly today's grammar.

After watching, record a 60-second description of a real trip you took, then describe a place you have always wanted to visit. Mix both tenses.

Lesson 9 -- On the Trail: Mont Blanc Preparation

Series / Documentary
14 Peaks: Nothing Is Impossible (Netflix)

A breathtaking documentary about high-altitude mountaineering. Perfect for hearing how climbers describe their gear, the trail, the summit, and how they plan and predict the future on the mountain.

Watch with English subtitles. Write two sentences with going to (their plans) and two with will (their predictions) about what you watched.

Podcast
The Tough Girl Podcast -- "Preparing for a Big Trek"

A clear, slow episode about getting ready for the mountains: choosing gear, packing a backpack, checking the forecast, and staying safe at altitude. Full of useful trekking vocabulary.

Pause and repeat each trekking phrase aloud. Note three new words and use each one in a sentence about your own future adventure.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "will vs going to"

A clear, practical video on the difference between will and going to: plans and evidence versus quick decisions, offers, and opinions. Exactly today's grammar.

After watching, record a 60-second plan for a future trip: what you are going to do, and what you think will happen. Mix both forms.

Lesson 10 -- The Story So Far: Progress Check

Series / Documentary
Down to Earth with Zac Efron (Netflix)

A relaxed travel series where the host reflects on what he has learned in each place he visits. Perfect for hearing real people mix past, present perfect, and future as they talk about their journeys.

Watch one episode with English subtitles. Write three sentences about it: one with the past simple, one with the present perfect, and one with going to or will.

Podcast
The Fluent Show -- "How to Track Your Language Progress"

A clear, encouraging episode about measuring your progress, setting goals, and staying confident as a language learner. Full of useful reflection language for talking about how far you have come.

After listening, record a one-minute voice note about your biggest achievement and your next goal in English. Use the words progress, improve, and confident.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "English Tenses Review"

A practical video that reviews the present simple, past simple, present perfect, and future together, with clear examples of when to use each one. Exactly today's grammar focus.

After watching, write four short sentences about your own story, one in each tense, and read them aloud while recording yourself.

Lesson 11 -- Getting Around: Public Transport

Series / Travel
Rick Steves' Europe -- City Transport Episodes (PBS / YouTube)

The host moves around big European cities using the subway, trams, and buses, explaining how to buy tickets, read maps, and transfer between lines. Full of natural advice with should and could.

Watch one episode with English subtitles. Write three sentences of advice for a tourist using should, could, and might.

Podcast
All Ears English -- "Travel English: Getting Around the City"

A short, clear episode about asking for directions and using public transport in English. You will hear the modal verbs should, could, and might used naturally in real travel situations.

After listening, record a one-minute voice note planning a trip across a city. Use the words subway, fare, transfer, and get off.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Modal Verbs: should, could, might"

A practical grammar video that explains how to give advice, offer options, and talk about possibilities, with clear examples. Exactly today's grammar focus.

After watching, write four sentences about your own city's transport, one with should, one with could, and two with might.

Lesson 12 -- Emergency! Health & Safety Abroad

Series / Travel
Wolters World -- "Travel Health: Staying Safe Abroad" (YouTube)

A friendly traveler explains how to find a pharmacy, describe your symptoms, and what to do in an emergency in another country. Full of natural first conditional and must / have to.

Watch one video with English subtitles. Write three sentences with if + will about staying healthy on a trip.

Podcast
All Ears English -- "At the Doctor and Pharmacy in English"

A short, clear episode about describing how you feel and asking for medicine in English. You will hear the words fever, painkiller, and symptom used in real situations.

After listening, record a one-minute voice note describing symptoms and asking a pharmacist for help. Use the words pharmacy, fever, painkiller, and medicine.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The First Conditional: if + present, will"

A practical grammar video that explains how to talk about real future possibilities and their results, with clear examples. Exactly today's grammar focus.

After watching, write four sentences about your own travel plans, each using if + present, will + base verb.

Lesson 13 -- Money Matters: Banks, Cash & Cards

Series / Travel
Wolters World -- "Money Tips for Travelers: Cash, Cards & Exchange" (YouTube)

A friendly traveler explains how to change money, use ATMs abroad, and avoid high fees and bad exchange rates. Full of natural money vocabulary in real situations.

Watch one video with English subtitles. Write three sentences with the second conditional about your dream trip (if + past, would).

Podcast
All Ears English -- "At the Bank and Changing Money in English"

A short, clear episode about handling money in English: changing currency, asking about fees, and withdrawing cash. You will hear the words exchange rate, fee, and balance used naturally.

After listening, record a one-minute voice note about changing money at a bank. Use the words bank, cash, exchange rate, and fee.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The Second Conditional: if + past, would"

A practical grammar video that explains how to talk about imaginary situations and their results, with clear examples. Exactly today's grammar focus.

After watching, write four sentences about imaginary plans, each using if + past, would + base verb. Try one with "if I were you".

Lesson 14 -- Making Plans: Reservations & Bookings

Series / Travel
Wolters World -- "How to Book a Hotel & Avoid Booking Mistakes" (YouTube)

A friendly traveler explains how to make a reservation, check availability, read the cancellation policy, and avoid booking mistakes. Full of natural booking vocabulary in real situations.

Watch one video with English subtitles. Write three sentences reporting what the video said, using "He said that..." and "He told us to...".

Podcast
All Ears English -- "Booking a Hotel and Making Reservations in English"

A short, clear episode about making plans in English: booking a room, asking about availability, and confirming or cancelling. You will hear the words reservation, deposit, and refund used naturally.

After listening, record a one-minute voice note reporting what the hosts said. Use said, told, and asked, plus the words booking, deposit, and deadline.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Reported Speech: said, told and backshift"

A practical grammar video that explains how to tell someone what another person said, with said, told, and the backshift of tenses. Exactly today's grammar focus.

After watching, write four reported sentences about a phone call, each one moving the verb one step back (have to had, will to would).

Lesson 15 -- Eating Out: Ordering & Polite Requests

Series / Travel
English with Lucy -- "Ordering Food in a Restaurant in English" (YouTube)

A clear, friendly guide to ordering at a restaurant in English: reading the menu, ordering each course, asking polite questions, and asking for the bill. Full of natural dining vocabulary.

Watch with English subtitles. Then write four polite requests you could use at a restaurant, two with "Could you..." and two with "Would you mind...".

Podcast
All Ears English -- "At the Restaurant: How to Order Politely in English"

A short, practical episode on dining out: ordering a meal, asking about the menu, making a polite complaint, and handling the bill. You will hear words like dish, course, and service charge used naturally.

After listening, record a one-minute voice note ordering a full meal for two. Use Could you, Would you mind, and at least four restaurant words.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Polite Requests: Could you...? and Would you mind...?"

A practical grammar video on making polite requests in English, with Could you + base verb and Would you mind + verb-ing. Exactly today's grammar focus.

After watching, write four polite requests for a restaurant, then change each "Could you..." into "Would you mind..." (remember the -ing).

Lesson 16 -- Out and About: Shopping & Returns

Series / Travel
English with Lucy -- "Shopping for Clothes in English: Sizes, Fitting Rooms & Returns" (YouTube)

A clear, friendly guide to shopping for clothes in English: asking for sizes, trying things on in the fitting room, talking about fit, and making a return. Full of natural shopping vocabulary.

Watch with English subtitles. Then write four sentences about clothes that do not fit, two with "too" and two with "not ... enough".

Podcast
All Ears English -- "At the Shops: Buying Clothes and Making Returns in English"

A short, practical episode on shopping: asking for a size, trying things on, asking about a discount, and returning a faulty item for an exchange or refund. You will hear words like size, fit, and discount used naturally.

After listening, record a one-minute voice note about a shopping trip. Use too, not enough, and at least four shopping words.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "too and enough: How to Use Them Correctly"

A practical grammar video on too + adjective and not + adjective + enough, with clear examples about sizes and fit. Exactly today's grammar focus.

After watching, write four sentences about clothes, then change each "too small" into "not big enough" (remember: enough goes after the adjective).
